journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

Analysis of 324 giant panda breeding events reveals that maternal age, gestational duration, and interbirth interval critically influence neonatal survival outcomes. Both very young dams (5–7 years) and advanced-age dams (≥20 years) experience elevated neonatal mortality, with optimal survival occurring at intermediate reproductive ages and intermediate gestational durations (110–127 days). Longer interbirth intervals (≥4 years) and appropriate spacing between breeding events allow sufficient maternal physiological recovery, reducing neonatal mortality, whereas short intervals (≤1 year) compromise maternal condition and elevate mortality risk. Birth weight substantially influences cub survival—singleton cubs weigh significantly more than twins and exhibit higher survival rates, while greater weight differences within twin pairs correlate with increased mortality. The variable gestational duration (71–188 days) primarily reflects embryonic diapause length regulated by progesterone, with birth weight determined instead by maternal nutrient allocation during active gestation rather than diapause duration. These findings demonstrate that multiple maternal and offspring factors synergistically affect reproductive success, with physiological maturity, adequate recovery time, and optimal developmental timing emerging as key determinants of panda cub survival.

title: “This Is My Future?”: Understanding the Lives of Emerging Adult Women Living with Chronic Pain Through a Narrative Inquiry
summary:
# Summary

This narrative inquiry study examined how emerging adult women (aged 18–39) experience chronic pain through the lens of gender, societal narratives, and healthcare interactions. Chronic pain affects up to 30% of individuals in this age group and disproportionately impacts women, who often face dismissal, disbelief, and attribution of their symptoms to psychological causes rather than legitimate medical conditions. The study followed two participants—Hailey, living with fibromyalgia, migraines, irritable bowel syndrome, and interstitial cystitis, and Megan, managing idiopathic scoliosis, adenomyosis, and endometriosis—documenting how family dismissal and healthcare provider skepticism shaped their pain identities and coping strategies. Both women internalized narratives that their pain was not credible or serious, leading to pain concealment, reduced healthcare engagement, and compromised psychological well-being. The findings underscore that gender bias in healthcare, combined with intergenerational and cultural expectations that women suppress complaints, creates barriers to adequate pain management and validation. Having a medically recognized diagnosis provided some improvement in healthcare credibility, yet the burden of appearing credible remained a persistent source of tension for these emerging adult women.

title: Global and China burden of hormone-related cancers and risk factors, 1990–2021: results from the Global Burden of Disease Study 2021
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease Study 2021 examined hormone-related cancers (HRCs) from 1990–2021, revealing that internal and external hormonal factors drive HRC development through multiple pathways: promoting cell growth, increasing sporadic genetic mutations, and enabling nuclear receptor binding to DNA target motifs that facilitate cancer progression. Key modifiable risk factors contributing to cancer mortality included smoking, alcohol consumption, low physical activity, and elevated BMI, with particularly strong associations between high BMI and female HRC risk. Age-specific incidence patterns showed female HRCs (breast, uterine, ovarian) peaking in the 60–74 age group globally and in China, while male cancers demonstrated distinct patterns, with testicular cancer prevalent in younger males (25–44 years) and prostate cancer in older males. Notably, China achieved significant mortality reductions for female HRCs between 2000–2015 (particularly uterine cancer with APC = -7.91), yet experienced increased mortality and prevalence for male breast, thyroid, and testicular cancers, highlighting divergent disease burden trends. The findings underscore that lifestyle factors, population aging, and hormonal mechanisms collectively drive HRC burden, necessitating age-stratified and cancer-type-specific prevention and management strategies.

title: Calcitonin gene-related peptide in newly diagnosed idiopathic intracranial hypertension: a prospective, cross-sectional, case-control study of cerebrospinal fluid and plasma
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: This prospective study investigated calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) levels in cerebrospinal fluid and plasma of patients with newly diagnosed idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) to determine whether CGRP contributes to the migraine-like headaches commonly associated with IIH. Although CGRP is well-established as a key mediator of migraine pain signaling through trigeminal activation, and monoclonal antibodies targeting CGRP are efficacious in treating both episodic and chronic migraine, the study found no significant differences in CGRP levels across plasma, cerebrospinal fluid, or their ratio between IIH patients, non-IIH headache controls, and healthy controls. Notably, CGRP levels in plasma correlated with CSF levels and were significantly higher in plasma than CSF, yet neither compartment showed associations with migraine phenotype, chronic headache, opening pressure, or body mass index in the IIH cohort. The findings suggest that despite the migraine-like phenotype of IIH-associated headaches, baseline CGRP dysregulation may not be a primary pathophysiologic mechanism in newly diagnosed IIH, though CGRP-targeted therapy remains an unexplored and potentially relevant treatment avenue for headache prevention in this population.

title: Magnitude and determinants of abnormal uterine bleeding among reproductive-age women in Kombolcha Rejiopolitan Administrative Town, Northeast Ethiopia: a FIGO-based study (2023)
summary: This Ethiopian cross-sectional study identified multiple determinants of abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) among reproductive-age women, with heavy menstrual bleeding representing the most common presentation at 22.7% of participants. Advanced reproductive age (41–49 years) emerged as the strongest predictor, conferring 4.2-fold increased odds of AUB, primarily through climacteric phase-induced declines in ovarian follicles and estradiol that disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Beyond age-related factors, hypertension (3.71-fold higher odds), prior abortion history (2.3-fold higher odds), anemia, and late menarche were independently associated with increased AUB risk. Pelvic pathologies including endometriosis and fibroids, which are particularly prevalent in perimenopausal women, emerged as additional causes of bleeding dysfunction. The study underscores that AUB constitutes a significant public health concern due to untreated complications including infertility, endometrial cancer, severe anemia, cardiovascular disease, and premature mortality, highlighting the clinical importance of identifying and managing these diverse etiologic factors.

title: Bias due to non-consent in assisted reproductive treatment cohort studies: consent for disclosure to non-contact research in the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Authority register
summary: This study demonstrates substantial consent bias in assisted reproductive technology (ART) cohort research, where patient willingness to disclose identifying information for research varies significantly by demographic and clinical characteristics. Older women (aged 40–44 years), ethnic minorities, and patients with previous ART treatments or live births showed lower consent-to-disclosure rates, with consent rates consistently higher in NHS-only funded clinics compared to private facilities. These differential consent patterns systematically altered outcome distributions: the consented cohort exhibited higher live birth rates and lower low birthweight prevalence, while younger women and those of white ethnicity demonstrated improved live birth rates and better neonatal outcomes overall. Using only consented data for analysis introduces bias toward more favorable outcomes, particularly overrepresenting successful cases from younger, White, previously successful patients. The findings highlight that naïve analyses of ART registry data failing to account for consent-related selection bias may yield misleading conclusions about treatment efficacy and safety. These biases are critical to consider when interpreting published ART outcomes and designing prospective studies involving sensitive health information.

title: CT of acute abdomen in the elderly
summary:
# Summary

This review examines acute abdominal pathology in elderly patients, where the disease spectrum differs markedly from younger populations. Bowel obstruction from colorectal cancer, sigmoid volvulus, and biliary disease represent the predominant emergencies in patients over 75 years, whereas appendicitis and gynecological conditions dominate in younger patients. Acute mesenteric ischemia (AMI) becomes ten times more common after age 80 and manifests with vague, atypical presentations including abdominal pain disproportionate to examination findings, vomiting, and diarrhea, with diagnostic delays directly responsible for mortality rates of 30-70%. CT imaging is critical for accurate diagnosis in elderly patients, as clinical assessment is complicated by altered mental status from medications, fever, electrolyte abnormalities, and blunted laboratory findings such as absent fever despite serious bacterial infection and normal liver function tests in cholecystitis. Additional age-related conditions including ischemic colitis (linked to hypertension, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease), fecal impaction, and volvulus associated with dementia and sedentary lifestyle further contribute to the high surgical burden in this population.

title: Alzheimer disease is (sometimes) highly heritable: Drivers of variation in heritability estimates for binary traits, a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Late-onset Alzheimer's disease (LOAD) is a complex neurodegenerative disorder characterized by memory loss and dementia, with heritability estimates varying substantially across studies due to methodological and population differences. Twin studies demonstrate the significant genetic contribution to LOAD risk, with monozygotic twins showing substantially higher concordance for dementia (0.86) compared to dizygotic twins (0.50), supporting additive genetic and unique environmental components in disease etiology. LOAD shows marked sex differences, disproportionately affecting females, with evidence suggesting that genetic components may manifest differently between males and females. Study design substantially influences heritability estimates, with family-based approaches yielding higher values than SNP-based methods using genome-wide data, and prevalent cases producing higher estimates than incident cases. Multiple estimation approaches—including Falconer's formula, structural equation modeling, and genome-based restricted maximum likelihood procedures—have been employed to partition genetic and environmental variance contributions. Overall, this systematic review highlights that LOAD heritability is dynamic, shaped by study population characteristics, age of onset, and methodological approaches, indicating the importance of standardized approaches when comparing heritability estimates across populations and studies.

title: United States racial/ethnic disparities in PGT-A use: an analysis of 2014–2020 SART CORS database
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of 2014-2020 assisted reproductive technology cycles reveals significant racial and ethnic disparities in preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A) utilization despite clear evidence that PGT-A improves cumulative live birth rates and reduces miscarriage rates in women 35 and older. Black and Hispanic women were substantially less likely to use PGT-A compared to White women (35% and 16% less likely, respectively), while Asian women were 41% more likely to utilize the technology. Contributing factors to these disparities include differential access to care, as cost represents a documented barrier for minority women, and potential provider bias in PGT-A counseling recommendations. Additionally, Black and Hispanic women presented for their first ART cycles at more advanced ages with higher body mass indices and lower ovarian reserve markers, yet paradoxically demonstrated lower PGT-A utilization rates despite greater clinical indications for testing. Although overall PGT-A adoption increased dramatically from 11.5% in 2014 to 49% in 2020—with Black women showing the steepest increase—persistent racial and ethnic gaps in access to this beneficial reproductive technology remain a critical equity concern.

title: Influence of Age on Treatment and Prognosis in Ovarian Cancer Patients
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how age influences treatment selection and outcomes in ovarian cancer patients, revealing significant disparities between younger and older cohorts. Patients aged 70 or older present with more advanced-stage disease (80.5% vs. 63% in stage III-IV) and have higher comorbidity burden, yet receive non-standard treatment more frequently and achieve complete cytoreduction less often (81.3% vs. 95.8%) compared to younger patients. These treatment differences substantially impact prognosis, with older patients experiencing significantly worse five-year overall survival (44.4% vs. 67.2%) despite no significant difference in disease-free survival. The standard treatment approach—complete cytoreductive surgery followed by platinum and taxane-based chemotherapy—remains the gold standard, though older patients more frequently undergo interval or rescue surgery due to health constraints. The study emphasizes that treatment decisions should be individualized based on overall health status rather than age alone, and suggests that geriatric screening tests and prehabilitation programs may optimize outcomes in older ovarian cancer patients.

title: Long-Term Effects of COVID-19 on Women’s Reproductive Health and Its Association with Autoimmune Diseases, Including Multiple Sclerosis
summary:
# Summary

SARS-CoV-2 infection directly compromises women's reproductive health by causing ovarian dysfunction, menstrual irregularities, and fertility challenges, with viral RNA detected in reproductive tissues including the ovaries and uterus. COVID-19 infection during pregnancy significantly increases risks for adverse outcomes including miscarriage, preterm birth, and fetal development issues through mechanisms involving placental inflammation and immune hyperactivation. Beyond acute reproductive effects, the virus triggers persistent post-viral immune dysregulation characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-6 and interferon-gamma), chronic inflammation, and molecular mimicry that collectively contribute to development and exacerbation of autoimmune diseases, particularly affecting women who show higher prevalence of long-term autoimmune complications including multiple sclerosis. Long COVID perpetuates these immunological and hormonal imbalances alongside neurological and constitutional symptoms, establishing a bidirectional relationship between viral infection, reproductive dysfunction, and autoimmune disease development in women of reproductive age.

title: Aspirin does not modify cardiovascular event risk in endometriosis in the California Teachers Study
summary:
# Summary

This study examined whether aspirin use modifies the elevated cardiovascular disease risk associated with endometriosis in a large cohort of female teachers. Endometriosis was independently associated with increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), particularly in women under 40 years of age, driven primarily by increased stroke and coronary heart disease risk rather than myocardial infarction. The elevated cardiovascular risk in endometriosis is likely mediated by systemic inflammatory pathways, including altered cytokine release and pro-angiogenic factors that promote endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerotic changes, potentially amplified by endometriosis-related pain and stress. However, regular aspirin use did not significantly modify the effect of endometriosis on MACE risk, nor did statin use, suggesting that conventional preventive strategies may not fully address the cardiovascular risk conferred by endometriosis. These findings highlight the need for alternative or complementary approaches to cardiovascular risk reduction in women with endometriosis beyond standard pharmaceutical interventions.

title: A phenome-wide association study of uterine fibroids reveals a marked burden of comorbidities
summary:
# Summary: Phenome-wide Association Study of Uterine Fibroids and Comorbidities

A comprehensive phenome-wide association study identified 389 conditions associated with uterine fibroid risk across multiple populations, revealing a substantial comorbidity burden extending beyond reproductive organs. Reproductive-related conditions showed the strongest associations, including endometriosis (odds ratio 7.89), irregular menstrual bleeding (8.85), dysmenorrhea (10.16), and excessive menstruation (12.16), alongside high associations with endometrial hyperplasia, ovarian cysts, and female infertility. Beyond gynecologic conditions, uterine fibroids were significantly associated with circulatory, dermatologic, genitourinary, musculoskeletal, and sense organ disorders, as well as metabolic conditions including obesity, lipid disorders, vitamin D deficiency, and thyroid nodules. Notable exceptions showed protective associations with conditions like ischemic heart disease, congestive heart failure, and peripheral vascular disease, suggesting complex cardiovascular interactions. Known risk factors including Black race, higher BMI, family history, hypertension, increasing age, and nulliparity were confirmed, establishing uterine fibroids as a condition with pervasive systemic health implications requiring comprehensive, multidisciplinary clinical assessment.

title: Pain in women: bridging the gender pain gap
summary: Women experience chronic pain at significantly higher rates than men, reporting an average of 1.6 painful events monthly compared to 1.2 in men, with heightened sensitivity to experimental pain stimuli such as heat, cold, and pressure. Multiple pain conditions show female preponderance, including migraines, tension headaches, fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, and rheumatoid arthritis, while female-specific pain conditions such as dysmenorrhea and endometriosis are associated with substantial comorbidities including headaches, migraines, backache, fatigue, nausea, and mood disturbances that collectively impact school attendance, sleep, mental health, and attention. Hormonal changes at puberty are implicated in both the onset of female-specific pains and the widening of the gender pain gap. Beyond biological mechanisms, societal factors including unconscious bias, stigma surrounding gynecological conditions, and attribution of women's pain to psychological causes contribute significantly to a gender pain gap in clinical care. Current evidence suggests some sex-dependent differences in analgesic efficacy, with morphine potentially showing greater analgesic effects in women, though sex-specific analyses remain limited for most common pain medications. Addressing this gap requires clinical approaches emphasizing empathy and compassionate curiosity to improve diagnosis and management of pain in women.

title: FSH receptor N680S genotype-guided gonadotropin choice increases cumulative pregnancy and live birth rates after in vitro fertilization
summary: The FSH receptor (FSHR) N680S polymorphism significantly influences gonadotropin responsiveness and IVF outcomes, with carriers of different genotypes showing distinct pharmacological preferences. S-allele carriers exhibit FSH resistance but display superior oocyte retrieval and pregnancy rates when treated with uFSH (urinary FSH), which has a longer half-life and generates higher cAMP production in S-variant receptors; conversely, NN-homozygous carriers show greater sensitivity to recombinant FSH (rFSH) and achieve better follicle development with this agent. A genotype-guided treatment approach, matching uFSH to S-allele carriers and rFSH to NN carriers, substantially improved clinical outcomes compared to conventional treatment protocols, with the optimally treated genotyped cohort achieving cumulative pregnancy rates of 51% versus 40% in non-genotyped controls and live birth rates of 40% versus 28%, respectively. These findings demonstrate that pharmacogenomic personalization of gonadotropin selection based on FSHR genotype represents a clinically meaningful strategy to enhance reproductive success in IVF, particularly relevant for endometriosis patients undergoing assisted reproduction who may have additional ovulatory challenges.

title: Knowledge, Attitude, and Practices of Clinicians Towards Involvement of Physiotherapists in the Management of Obstetrics and Gynaecological Conditions: A Cross‐Sectional Study
summary: This cross-sectional study examined clinicians' knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding physiotherapy involvement in obstetric and gynecological care in Ghana. Physiotherapy is recognized as an effective intervention for a broad spectrum of conditions including pelvic organ prolapse, dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction, urinary and fecal incontinence, and muscle weakness, with the potential to prevent, manage, and rehabilitate obstetric and gynecological conditions that significantly impact women's physical and psychological well-being. While clinicians in Ghana demonstrated general awareness of physiotherapy's role in obstetric and gynecological practice, they exhibited limited specific knowledge about which conditions respond to physiotherapy treatment. Senior clinicians such as specialists and consultants showed substantially higher knowledge levels compared to residents and medical officers, and prior exposure to physiotherapy during training and previous interprofessional experience with physiotherapists were strong predictors of patient referral practices. The study identified several barriers to physiotherapy utilization, including low awareness of its role in specific conditions like pelvic inflammatory disease and post-hysterectomy rehabilitation, inadequate labor ward resources, and concerns about interprofessional relationships. Enhancing clinician education about physiotherapy's scope during medical training and increasing the availability of physiotherapy units near discharged patients could improve service utilization and patient outcomes.

title: Menopausal hormone therapy and the risk of systemic lupus erythematosus and systemic sclerosis: a population-based nested case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based nested case-control study demonstrates that menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) significantly increases the risk of both systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and systemic sclerosis (SSc), with effects mediated through exogenous estrogen exposure. MHT use was associated with 30% increased odds of SLE and 40% increased odds of SSc, with the highest risks observed in women using combined systemic and local formulations (odds ratios of 1.9 and 1.8, respectively) or systemic estrogen-progestogen combinations. The timing of MHT initiation critically influenced disease risk, with women starting MHT between ages 46-50 years exhibiting the highest odds of SLE development. Mechanistically, estrogen promotes SLE through multiple pathways including toll-like receptor signaling, altered cytokine secretion, enhanced autoantigen presentation, and autoantibody production, while potentially influencing SSc through endothelin interactions. These findings have important clinical implications for postmenopausal women at risk for these autoimmune conditions, suggesting careful consideration of MHT risks despite symptom management benefits.

title: How Do Men Who Post Publicly on Social Media Author Themselves and Their Experiences of Crohn’s Disease? A Dialogical Analysis of Three Cases
summary: This qualitative study examines how three men with Crohn's disease construct narratives about their illness experiences through public social media posting. Crohn's disease causes chronic inflammation and ulceration of the gut lining, resulting in symptoms including severe abdominal pain, diarrhea, fatigue, and weight loss, creating unpredictability that significantly impacts patients' lives. The research reveals that hegemonic masculine ideals discourage help-seeking behavior and that men with inflammatory bowel disease demonstrate poorer coping skills and medication adherence compared to women. However, online environments provide therapeutic value by enabling emotional expression about illness experiences, with blogging specifically reducing emotional distress in affected men. The three case studies demonstrate distinct narrative strategies—including epic authorship that constructs certainty and control over future health challenges, and cyclical time concepts reflecting patterns of remission and flare-ups—that allow men to author themselves and regain agency in the face of a chronic, unpredictable disease. These findings highlight how social media platforms can serve as spaces where men overcome traditional masculine barriers to emotional disclosure and develop coping mechanisms for managing Crohn's disease.

title: Using the PowerMom Digital Health Platform to Support Prenatal Mental Health and Maternal Health Outcomes: Observational Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Anxiety and depression are highly prevalent among individuals of childbearing age and significantly influence pregnancy-related symptoms and outcomes. This observational cohort study using the PowerMom digital health platform found that pregnant individuals receiving treatment for anxiety or depression reported substantially higher rates of physical symptoms during pregnancy, including fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain, body aches, headaches, and vaginal bleeding, as well as a higher prevalence of pre-existing conditions such as endometriosis, hypertension, and eating disorders. Notably, treated participants experienced worse postpartum mental health outcomes, with 45.8% at high risk for perinatal mood disorder compared to untreated participants, and demonstrated significantly higher postpartum mental health composite scores indicating more severe psychological distress. Additionally, prenatal depression emerged as a strong predictor of postpartum depressive symptoms, while antenatal anxiety was associated with perinatal depression, underscoring the bidirectional relationship between prenatal and postpartum mental health. Treated participants also showed higher rates of adverse pregnancy outcomes, including miscarriage (14.8%), stillbirth (4.3%), and epidural use (76.3%), suggesting that prenatal mental health conditions have complex associations with both maternal physical symptoms and birth outcomes.

title: The Uterus Keeps the Score: Black Women Academics’ Insights and Coping with Uterine Fibroids
journal: Journal of Health and Social Behavior
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids, which affect up to 80% of Black women and are disproportionately prevalent among Black women academics, produce a constellation of disruptive symptoms including abnormal menstrual bleeding, pelvic pressure, bowel dysfunction, urinary frequency, low back pain, constipation, and dyspareunia. Left untreated, fibroids significantly increase risks of cesarean delivery, breech pregnancy, preterm delivery, postpartum hemorrhage, and infertility, while also causing secondary iron deficiencies and emotional distress. Treatment options range from noninvasive approaches such as hormonal contraceptives, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications, and acupuncture to surgical interventions including myomectomy and hysterectomy. Among Black women academics specifically, the experience of fibroid-related illness is compounded by endorsement of superwoman schema—a coping mechanism characterized by excessive work habits and strength demonstration—which leads to postponement of medical treatment, depression, anxiety, poor sleep, and hypertension risk, while tenure-track demands and academic stress further exacerbate symptoms. Risk factors including high body mass index, vitamin D deficiency, elevated cholesterol, and occupational stress contribute to fibroid development and progression in this population. The study reveals that symptomatic fibroids substantially reduce work productivity and generate profound psychosocial burdens including feelings of helplessness and pain normalization among affected Black women faculty.

title: The impact of obesity and weight loss treatment on metabolic parameters, cardiovascular autonomic and sensory nerve function and in vitro fertilization outcomes in infertile women: a pilot study
summary: This pilot study examined how obesity and weight loss interventions affect infertile women's metabolic health, nerve function, and fertility outcomes. Obesity emerged as a significant cause of multiple pathological conditions, including neuropathy, peripheral sensory and autonomic dysfunction, and reproductive impairment, while obese patients demonstrated elevated blood pressure, metabolic disorder prevalence, and reduced cardiovascular autonomic function markers. Weight loss therapy combined with lifestyle modification and liraglutide (a GLP-1 receptor agonist) substantially improved metabolic parameters and reduced body weight, with prior studies showing such interventions increase pregnancy and live birth rates while decreasing the number of IVF cycles required. The GLP-1 receptor agonist liraglutide offered dual benefits of treating obesity while providing documented cardiovascular protection relevant to women undergoing fertility treatment. These findings support preconceptional weight loss as a critical intervention for obese infertile women, particularly those with conditions like PCOS, addressing both metabolic dysfunction and reproductive outcomes simultaneously.

title: Whole-genome sequencing and comparative genomic analysis of Irpex lacteus isolated from the female reproductive tract
summary:
# Summary

This study reports the whole-genome sequencing of *Irpex lacteus* strain BJFC24, a wood-degrading fungus unusually isolated from the cervix uteri of a patient with both endometriosis and COVID-19 infection. The BJFC24 strain retains lignocellulose-degrading capabilities while encoding 193 glycoside hydrolase genes and 24 secondary metabolite clusters that may facilitate adaptation to the human reproductive tract environment. Comparative genomic analysis reveals that human-derived strains like BJFC24 possess enhanced virulence-associated genes and pathways involved in signal transduction, carbohydrate metabolism, and post-translational modification, distinguishing them from environmental isolates that retain greater metabolic versatility for polysaccharide degradation. The identification of flavone/flavonol and peptidoglycan biosynthesis pathways unique to reproductive tract-colonizing strains suggests specific molecular mechanisms enabling fungal establishment in this niche. These findings establish *I. lacteus* as an emerging opportunistic pathogen potentially contributing to endometriosis comorbidities, warranting further investigation of its role in reproductive tract dysbiosis and pathogenesis.

title: The emergence of common health conditions across the life course: evidence from the Born in Bradford family cohort
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study from the Born in Bradford research program reveals striking ethnic differences in disease incidence patterns between South Asian and White European women across the lifespan. South Asian women demonstrated significantly elevated incidence of metabolic and reproductive conditions, including diabetes (nearly four-fold higher), chronic liver disease (three-fold higher), thyroid disorders, polycystic ovarian syndrome, uterine fibroids, and infertility, but notably had lower rates of endometriosis compared to White European women. In contrast, White European women exhibited higher incidence of mental health disorders, cancer, neuromuscular conditions, and fibromyalgia. The elevated diabetes and chronic liver disease burden in South Asian populations appears linked to higher hepatic fat accumulation and metabolic risk factors including obesity, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance, while hypothyroidism may be partially attributable to lower iodine intake. These findings highlight the importance of ethnicity-specific disease surveillance and prevention strategies, particularly for endometriosis where the inverse ethnic pattern warrants further investigation into biological, genetic, and healthcare-seeking behavioral factors.

title: Comparative reproductive pathology, a walk on the wild side
summary:
# Summary

This comparative analysis examines reproductive pathologies across species, with particular relevance to understanding endometriosis and adenomyosis in humans. Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue and involving retrograde menstruation as a key pathogenic mechanism, leads to infertility and chronic pelvic pain in affected women and has been documented spontaneously only in non-human primates including great apes and Afroeurasian monkeys. Adenomyosis, involving migration of endometrial glands into the myometrium, is promoted by factors such as peristalsis, pregnancy, and curettage, and occurs at higher prevalence in parous women and certain animal species like African painted dogs, where it can result in severe complications including uterine rupture. The study reveals that endometrial hyperplasia, accelerated by exogenous progestin exposure, similarly leads to infertility and subfertility depending on lesion severity. Additionally, the analysis highlights broader reproductive cancer risks associated with hormonal therapies and genetic factors, such as BRCA mutations in jaguars predisposing to multiple reproductive malignancies, and infectious agents like herpesviruses and treponemal species contributing to urogenital pathology and infertility across species.

title: Prevalence and patterns of pre-existing multimorbidity in pregnancy in Northern Ireland: a population-based, retrospective study using linked routinely collected healthcare data
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study examined the prevalence and patterns of multimorbidity in pregnant women in Northern Ireland, finding that nearly one-quarter of pregnant women had multiple pre-existing conditions affecting their health during pregnancy. Maternal obesity, socioeconomic deprivation, advanced maternal age, and higher gravida were all significantly associated with increased multimorbidity, with obesity showing particularly strong effects (ranging from 18.37% in healthy BMI to 39.18% in obesity class III). Mental health conditions emerged as the most prevalent organ system involved in multimorbidity (18.6–26.2%), with disproportionate impacts on younger women under 25 years and underweight women. While respiratory and dermatological conditions also contributed substantially to multimorbidity patterns, women with multimorbidity demonstrated significantly elevated risks of adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm birth, alongside increased likelihood of polypharmacy, disability, and mortality. The data underscore that multimorbidity represents a major clinical challenge in pregnancy management, particularly among vulnerable populations including those from socioeconomically deprived areas.

title: Tailoring cardiovascular risk prediction to females
summary:
# Summary

Cardiovascular risk in females requires sex-tailored prediction models, as females experience lower absolute cardiovascular disease risk than males but face distinct risk trajectories and comorbidities that traditional assessment tools may not adequately capture. Biomarkers such as cardiac troponin are persistently lower in females across the lifespan, yet increase with age in both sexes, necessitating sex-specific reference ranges. Type 2 diabetes confers a disproportionately higher relative CVD risk in females compared to males, potentially explained by differences in body fat distribution and metabolic responses. Reproductive and gynecological conditions—including polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, premature menopause, and adverse pregnancy outcomes such as gestational diabetes and pre-eclampsia—significantly elevate cardiovascular risk in women. Gynecological procedures including hysterectomy and bilateral oophorectomy are associated with increased ASCVD risk, while combined hormonal contraceptives and hormone replacement therapy increase stroke and ischemic heart disease risk. Comprehensive female-specific risk assessment must integrate traditional factors, female-prevalent conditions, pregnancy-related complications, socioeconomic deprivation, and reproductive history to accurately identify and treat cardiovascular disease risk in women.

title: Acceptance rates and reasons for social oocyte cryopreservation among women: systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examines acceptance rates and motivations for social oocyte cryopreservation (egg freezing) among women, finding that over half of women in the general population accept this fertility preservation method. The technique addresses age-related fertility decline and can treat conditions such as endometriosis by preserving reproductive capacity at younger ages before fertility decreases. Acceptance of oocyte cryopreservation is driven by multiple interconnected factors: women with career aspirations and educational goals, those lacking suitable partners, and those with concerns about age-related fertility loss show higher acceptance rates, particularly when procedures are cost-covered or supported by state funding. Notably, acceptance rates increase from 56.5% in the general population to 67.9% when costs are covered, while healthcare professionals and students show lower acceptance rates (42-66%), and cultural and knowledge gaps significantly reduce uptake in certain regions such as Lebanon. The growing adoption of this technology presents important implications for health policy, gender roles, and workplace practices, with acceptance influenced by personal factors including confidence in procedure safety and child health outcomes, alongside social and economic considerations including partner status and financial accessibility.

title: The quality and reliability of short videos about premature ovarian failure on Bilibili and TikTok: Cross-sectional study
summary: Premature ovarian failure, a condition causing loss of ovarian function before age 40 that results in cessation of menstruation and infertility, is associated with multiple serious comorbidities including hot flashes, osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, anxiety, and depression. Short-video platforms like TikTok and Bilibili have become major channels for disseminating health information about POF, though this cross-sectional analysis found that overall video quality and reliability on both platforms are unsatisfactory. Videos uploaded by healthcare professionals demonstrated significantly higher quality and scientific accuracy scores than user-generated content, while TikTok videos showed higher engagement metrics and slightly better quality than Bilibili videos; however, user engagement did not correlate with video quality on either platform. The most frequently discussed topic was POF symptoms at 29% of videos, while diagnosis and treatment received minimal coverage at 16% and 7.4% respectively, with some videos making exaggerated effectiveness claims for unproven treatments like herbal medicine and massage. On Bilibili specifically, longer video duration was positively associated with better quality and reliability scores, whereas TikTok's popularity did not translate to scientific rigor. These findings underscore the need for improved health information quality on social media platforms to ensure accurate POF education for the public.

title: Bladder capacity as a benchmark for patient stratification in interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study establishes anesthetic bladder capacity (BC) as a clinically useful benchmark for stratifying interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) patients into distinct phenotypes. Patients with low BC (≤500 cc) demonstrate a bladder-centric disease profile characterized by older age, higher symptom severity scores, increased prevalence of Hunner lesions, and a distinctive gene expression pattern associated with chronic bladder inflammation. In contrast, patients with normal or high BC (>500 cc) present a non-bladder-centric phenotype with significantly greater burden of non-urologic associated symptoms, including endometriosis, irritable bowel syndrome, fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, and depression. This BC-based stratification identifies two molecularly and clinically distinct IC/BPS subgroups with different disease mechanisms, demographic characteristics, and comorbidity profiles, with low BC patients clustering together regardless of Hunner lesion status and showing shorter symptom duration before diagnosis. These findings suggest that BC measurement could guide patient stratification and potentially enable more targeted therapeutic approaches in IC/BPS management.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of uterine cancer among women aged 50 years and older from 1990 to 2021: a systematic analysis for the global burden of disease study 2021
journal: Journal of Health, Population, and Nutrition
summary: This global burden of disease analysis reveals that uterine cancer represents an increasingly significant health threat, particularly among women aged 50 and older, with incidence strongly linked to age, menopause, genetic predisposition, abnormal hormone levels, and obesity. Multiple modifiable risk factors contribute to disease development, including sedentary lifestyle, high dietary intake of ultra-processed foods, and elevated circulating estrogen levels, while protective factors include physical activity, antioxidant-rich diets, and reduced dietary fat with increased fiber intake. Geographic disparities are pronounced, with high-income regions in North America, Europe, and developed countries experiencing substantially higher incidence and mortality rates compared to less developed areas, likely reflecting differences in socioeconomic status and lifestyle factors. Population growth emerged as the primary driver of disability-adjusted life years (DALYs), accounting for 132.55% of changes and substantially outweighing epidemiological and aging factors. Dietary modifications that reduce circulating estrogen—including increased fiber intake and decreased fat consumption—along with enhanced physical activity represent key preventive strategies. These findings underscore the importance of implementing region-specific public health interventions targeting modifiable risk factors, particularly in high-income nations experiencing the fastest growth in uterine cancer cases.

title: The Impact of Social, Cultural, and Identity-Related Factors on Delayed Childbearing: A Multi-Center Study
summary:
# Summary

This multi-center study examined how social, cultural, and identity-related factors influence delayed childbearing decisions in women. Reproductive health issues, educational attainment, full-time employment, and career advancement aspirations were the strongest predictors, with reproductive health issues increasing the likelihood of delayed childbearing by 2.70-fold and each educational level corresponding to a 2.1-year delay in childbearing age. Career advancement was cited by 71% of participants as the primary reason for postponement, followed by financial security concerns (61%) and age-related anxiety (44%), while paradoxically, age-related anxiety also motivated earlier reproductive decision-making in some cases. Delayed childbearing was associated with increased risks of fertility challenges, pregnancy complications, preterm birth, and low birth weight, and collectively contributed to declining fertility rates and population aging. The findings underscore that women's increasing access to education and professional opportunities, combined with psychological barriers including fear of losing autonomy and concerns about balancing motherhood with career ambitions, drive the trend toward later childbearing across multiple socioeconomic strata. These results highlight the complex interplay between women's aspirations for self-fulfillment and the biological and societal consequences of reproductive postponement.

title: The effect of stellate ganglion block on postoperative recovery quality in peri-menopausal women undergoing gynecological laparoscopic surgery
summary:
# Summary

Preoperative stellate ganglion block (SGB) using ropivacaine significantly improves postoperative recovery quality in perimenopausal women undergoing gynecological laparoscopic surgery by reducing sympathetic nerve excitability and stabilizing hemodynamics. The SGB-treated group demonstrated substantially lower postoperative pain scores at 4, 8, and 12 hours, along with reduced incidence of common adverse effects including nausea, vomiting, headache, shoulder pain, and abdominal distension. Gastrointestinal function recovery was accelerated, as evidenced by shorter times to first flatus and return of bowel sounds, while intraoperative hemodynamic stability was maintained with lower heart rate and mean arterial pressure fluctuations. The overall quality of recovery, assessed via QoR-40 scores, was significantly higher in the SGB group across emotional state, physical comfort, psychological support, and self-care ability domains. These benefits are particularly relevant for perimenopausal patients who experience hormonal fluctuations and autonomic nervous system dysfunction, conditions exacerbated by CO₂-induced sympathetic activation during laparoscopic surgery.

title: Abstracts from the 18 th European Headache Congress (EHC)
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary:
# Summary

This European Headache Congress abstract collection addresses comorbidities between headache disorders and endometriosis-related conditions, with particular focus on analgesic effects and neuroendocrine mechanisms. Frequent over-the-counter analgesic use, particularly acetaminophen, significantly inhibits granulosa cell function and de novo DNA synthesis, leading to suppressed anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels; notably, AMH levels recover by 21% following analgesic withdrawal, suggesting reversible ovarian suppression. The calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) pathway emerges as a critical mechanism in migraine pathophysiology, with CGRP activating vasodilation through CLR-RAMP1 and CLR-RAMP2 receptors, and young women showing elevated RAMP2 with downregulated RAMP1 expression. Migraine prevalence is highest in pre-menopausal women, and physical activity (cycling, walking, running) reduces migraine frequency while sedentary behavior and environmental triggers increase attacks. Additionally, idiopathic intracranial hypertension shows elevated inflammatory markers (leukocytes, neutrophils, alkaline phosphatase) correlating with increased intracranial pressure and papilledema, while chronic cluster headache exhibits elevated thalamic myo-inositol levels.

title: Trust in healthcare and perceived discrimination in Sweden: a fixed effects analysis of individual heterogeneity and discriminatory accuracy
summary:
# Summary

This Swedish population study demonstrates that perceived discrimination is a major driver of reduced trust in healthcare systems, with profound implications for health equity and patient outcomes. The research reveals a strong bidirectional relationship: individuals who experience discrimination—whether based on race, gender, disability, or other grounds—subsequently develop significantly lower trust in healthcare providers, while those with low healthcare trust report worse health outcomes and psychological distress. Notably, the effect of discrimination on trust is remarkably consistent across diverse demographic groups, though specific high-risk populations emerged, including foreign-born individuals with lower education and those who report not being taken seriously by medical professionals. The study found that trust in healthcare was lowest (>50%) among foreign-born individuals aged 25–64 years who had experienced discrimination, regardless of education level, whereas trust remained highest among native-born, older, educated individuals without discrimination experiences. These findings underscore how healthcare discrimination perpetuates health inequities by eroding patient trust, which in turn compromises engagement with medical care and overall health outcomes, suggesting that addressing discriminatory practices in healthcare settings is essential for improving population health equity.

title: Sex and Age Differences in Ketamine Efficacy and Safety in Chronic Pain Alleviation
summary: Ketamine, an N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonist, effectively treats chronic pain, depression, and suicidal ideation through inhibition of excitatory synaptic transmission. Women present with significantly higher baseline pain intensity, anxiety, and depression compared to men, and endometriosis and fibromyalgia show strong female predominance as chronic pain comorbidities. While repeated ketamine administration shows equivalent efficacy across sexes for pain and mood disorders, single-dose ketamine appears more effective in men, particularly younger men. Women experience higher adverse event rates (19% versus 13% in men), including headaches, fatigue, urinary infections, and sweating, correlating with elevated concentrations of ketamine metabolites such as dehydronorketamine and hydroxynorketamine. These sex-based differences in metabolism are attributed to age-related changes in cytochrome P450 enzyme activity (CYP2B6 and CYP3A4), which influence ketamine clearance and metabolite accumulation. The findings suggest that repeated dosing protocols may be preferable for women to achieve therapeutic benefits while minimizing safety concerns associated with sex-dependent pharmacokinetics.

title: Coverage Gaps and Contraceptive Use Among Medicare Enrollees With Disabilities
summary: This study demonstrates that Medicare's limited contraceptive coverage creates significant barriers to contraceptive use among disabled beneficiaries compared to other US insurance programs. Women enrolled in traditional Medicare had the lowest contraceptive use (4.9%), while those with dual Medicare-Medicaid coverage—which provides comprehensive contraceptive coverage without cost-sharing—achieved the highest rates (13.1%), indicating that financial access is a critical determinant of contraceptive use. Out-of-pocket costs for contraceptives consistently reduced use across all Medicare plans, with women unable to afford methods being twice as likely to discontinue use compared to their non-disabled counterparts. The transition from Medicare-only to dual enrollment increased overall contraceptive use by 3.9 percentage points, with the largest gains in short-acting methods (2.6 percentage points) followed by long-acting reversible methods (1.4 percentage points), demonstrating that removing cost barriers substantially increases adoption. These findings reveal that Medicare stands alone among major US insurance programs in lacking comprehensive contraceptive coverage, perpetuating disparities in reproductive autonomy for the 60 million disabled enrollees. The authors recommend that the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services and Congress mandate Medicare coverage of all FDA-approved contraceptive methods without cost-sharing to eliminate this inequitable barrier to desired contraceptive use.

title: Generational differences in associations between health conditions in young women and BMI categories
summary: This study examined associations between BMI categories and multiple health conditions in young women across two generational cohorts (1973-1978 and 1989-1995). Both overweight and obesity showed strong associations with cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and cancers, establishing weight as a significant risk factor across metabolic and oncologic outcomes. Beyond these traditional obesity-related conditions, the research identified broad associations between BMI extremes and diverse health problems including depression, back pain, urinary incontinence, and menstrual irregularities (irregular periods, heavy bleeding, severe pain, and premenstrual tension). Age emerged as the dominant factor for endometriosis and miscarriage risk, while modifiable lifestyle factors like smoking and physical activity consistently influenced health outcomes—with current smoking elevating disease risk and increased physical activity reducing the odds of poor self-rated health, depression, and back pain. Notably, the younger cohort (1989-1995) demonstrated higher prevalence of multiple conditions including menstrual problems, depression, and urinary symptoms compared to the older cohort, suggesting either generational differences in disease burden or diagnostic/reporting patterns. These findings underscore that BMI and lifestyle factors collectively influence a spectrum of health conditions in young women, with implications for comprehensive clinical assessment of endometriosis and its comorbidities.

title: The development and application of mini-barcodes from mitochondrial DNA for identifying medicinal leeches from traditional medicines
summary:
# Summary

This study develops and validates a mini-barcoding approach using mitochondrial DNA to reliably identify medicinal leech species used in traditional Chinese medicine. Three leech species listed in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia—Whitmania pigra, Whitmania acranulata, and Hirudo nipponia—were characterized, with the ND1F1/R1 primer pair achieving optimal amplification results for species identification. The mini-barcoding method effectively overcomes DNA degradation problems common in processed medicinal products, enabling accurate authentication where conventional full-length DNA barcoding fails. Analysis of commercial leech-derived products revealed significant species mislabeling, with most products containing Whitmania pigra despite label claims of other species, and one product incorrectly labeled as Hirudo nipponia. These findings demonstrate that mini-barcoding provides a reliable quality control tool for ensuring product authenticity and safety in traditional medicine supply chains. The diverse bioactive compounds secreted by different leech species—including anticoagulants, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic substances—underscore the clinical importance of accurate species identification for therapeutic efficacy.

title: Body roundness index and mental health in middle-aged and elderly adults: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study demonstrates that body roundness index (BRI), a measure of central adiposity, is strongly associated with increased risks of mental disorders in middle-aged and elderly adults. Higher BRI was significantly associated with overall mental disorders, depressive disorder (34% increased risk), anxiety disorder, and substance use disorder, with particularly pronounced effects observed in individuals younger than 60 years and in females across multiple depressive symptoms. The study identifies a mechanistic pathway linking BRI to psychiatric symptoms through white matter integrity alterations in key brain tracts including the forceps minor, anterior thalamic radiation, and superior longitudinal fasciculus, suggesting that central obesity-induced neuroinflammation via pro-inflammatory mediators affects neural connectivity. Additionally, high BRI correlated with specific symptom clusters such as fatigue, poor appetite or overeating (2.45-fold increased risk), concentration difficulties, and anhedonia. These findings suggest that BRI serves as a clinically valuable somatic biomarker for identifying vulnerability to mental disorders and highlight the importance of central adiposity assessment in mental health screening and intervention strategies.

title: Sex differences in endocrine, metabolic and psychological disturbance in obese patients with OSA
summary:
# Summary

Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is strongly associated with glucolipid metabolic disruption, endocrine disturbances, and psychological distress, with nocturnal hypoxia emerging as a central mechanism linking OSA to insulin resistance, thyroid hormone dysregulation, and pituitary-adrenal axis activation. The study reveals marked sex differences in how OSA manifests: men with severe OSA experience greater daytime sleepiness associated with nocturnal hypoxia, while women with severe OSA exhibit lower insulin sensitivity, elevated thyroid hormones, increased adrenal axis activation, and higher anxiety levels. In women specifically, metabolic and hormonal disruptions from nocturnal hypoxia occur independently of standard sleep parameters, with oxygen desaturation index (ODI) directly predicting insulin resistance and midnight cortisol levels associated with impaired insulin sensitivity. These sex-differentiated pathophysiological responses suggest that women and men with OSA require tailored clinical approaches to address their distinct metabolic, endocrine, and psychological comorbidities, particularly given the relevance to endometriosis-related metabolic and hormonal dysfunction.

title: Increased risk of uterine leiomyoma among women with migraine in reproductive age
summary: This study reveals a previously unexplored association between migraine and increased risk of uterine leiomyoma in reproductive-age women, with vitamin D deficiency emerging as a potential common pathophysiological link. Both conditions share inflammatory and hormonal underpinnings: vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in migraine patients and correlates with increased leiomyoma risk, while the active metabolite 1,25(OH)2D3 inhibits leiomyoma cell proliferation and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines implicated in neurogenic inflammation underlying migraine. Notably, migraine with aura carries a higher incidence of leiomyoma development than migraine without aura, suggesting distinct disease mechanisms. The study identifies estrogen and progesterone as critical drivers of myoma growth, with increased lifetime estrogen exposure—from early menarche, late menopause, and obesity—further elevating leiomyoma risk. Multiple comorbidities including hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, and endometriosis were significantly more common in women with leiomyoma, indicating shared pathophysiological pathways. These findings suggest that vitamin D supplementation may represent a therapeutic strategy to address both conditions simultaneously through anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative mechanisms.

title: Symptoms and objective signs of peripheral sensory neuropathy in POTS and correlations to gastrointestinal symptoms
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes the neurological manifestations of postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS), a disorder involving cardiovascular autonomic dysfunction that extends to both the peripheral and enteric nervous systems. Patients with POTS exhibited significantly higher prevalence and severity of peripheral sensory neuropathy symptoms and gastrointestinal symptoms compared to controls, with these manifestations closely associated within the patient population. A mechanistic subtype, neuropathic POTS, involves small nerve fiber neuropathy characterized by degeneration of thinly myelinated Aδ-fibers and unmyelinated C-fibers, which can affect somatic, autonomic, and enteric nervous systems; objective findings in some POTS patients include reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density and postganglionic sudomotor dysfunction reflecting partial sympathetic denervation. Notable comorbidities in this POTS cohort included Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, irritable bowel syndrome, and endometriosis (11.6%), with gastrointestinal symptoms in POTS patients resembling those seen in irritable bowel syndrome and gastroparesis. The close association between neuropathic symptoms and gastrointestinal dysfunction suggests shared pathophysiological mechanisms involving enteric nerve involvement, highlighting the multi-system nature of POTS pathology.

title: Effect of oral contraceptive consumption timing on substrate metabolism, cognition, and exercise performance in females: a randomised controlled trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that oral contraceptive consumption acutely modulates substrate metabolism and cognitive function in females through hormonal mechanisms. Oral contraceptive ingestion significantly reduced the respiratory exchange ratio (RER) at both 70% and 90% lactate threshold, indicating a shift toward greater fat oxidation and reduced carbohydrate utilization during exercise, consistent with the metabolic effects of exogenous estrogen observed naturally across the menstrual cycle. Simultaneously, oral contraceptive consumption enhanced verbal fluency performance while reducing arousal and subjective feeling scales during exercise, effects mediated by estrogen's influence on cognitive function through cholinergic and monoaminergic pathways. The synthetic estrogen ethinylestradiol demonstrated greater affinity for estrogen receptors than natural estrogen, contributing to these pronounced metabolic and cognitive effects. These findings highlight the complex interplay between reproductive hormones and female physiology during exercise, with potential implications for understanding how hormonal contraceptives affect performance and well-being in females engaging in physical activity.

title: A moonshot for female astronaut health, a win for all astronauts and space exploration
summary: Spaceflight exposure poses significant reproductive health risks that disproportionately affect female astronauts, including abnormal uterine bleeding, vaginal microbiome disruption, and associations with endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids through mechanisms involving the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Microgravity and radiation exposure during spaceflight can damage ovarian function and increase the risk of premature ovarian insufficiency, which subsequently elevates the risk of cardiovascular disease and osteoporosis—conditions with long-term health consequences. Female astronauts experience higher rates of post-flight orthostatic intolerance and cardiovascular changes compared to their male counterparts, suggesting sex-based physiological differences in spaceflight adaptation. Hormonal countermeasures used to mitigate some spaceflight effects carry their own risks, potentially increasing abnormal uterine bleeding, anemia, ovarian cyst formation, osteopenia, and venous thromboembolism. The article highlights critical gaps in scientific understanding regarding how the spaceflight environment comprehensively affects female reproductive health, warranting urgent investigation to ensure safe exploration for all astronauts while advancing broader knowledge applicable to women's health on Earth.

title: Menstrual bleeding-specific quality of life in women on antiplatelet therapy
journal: Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis
summary: This study examined menstrual bleeding outcomes in women receiving antiplatelet therapy for cardiovascular disease prevention. While antiplatelet drugs are known to increase overall bleeding risks, this research found that menstrual blood loss and menstrual bleeding-specific quality of life were comparable between women on antiplatelet therapy and controls, with no significant increase in heavy menstrual bleeding attributable to these medications. Notably, 42% of antiplatelet drug users reported a history of iron deficiency and/or anemia, and patients with prior stroke or transient ischemic attack comprised 42% of the treatment group, indicating that cardiovascular disease rather than medication-induced bleeding may account for hematologic complications. Contraceptive use emerged as a critical modifier, with women not using hormonal contraception showing substantially higher menstrual bleeding burden scores in both groups. The findings suggest that while anticoagulants are definitively associated with heavy menstrual bleeding and quality of life impacts, antiplatelet therapy does not substantially worsen menstrual outcomes in reproductive-age women, supporting their continued use for stroke and myocardial infarction prevention when clinically indicated.

title: Ethno-Racial Differences in Age and Symptom Severity Among Pre-Menopausal Women Commencing Treatment for Benign Gynecological Conditions with a Levonorgestrel-Releasing Intrauterine Device
summary: This study reveals significant ethno-racial disparities in the presentation and treatment of benign gynecological conditions among women receiving levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices (LNG-IUDs). Black and Hispanic patients presented with substantially more severe uterine bleeding and bulk symptoms—approximately 1.5 times greater severity than White patients—and were more likely to have abnormal uterine bleeding and fibroid diagnoses. Black women demonstrated earlier onset and higher prevalence of uterine fibroids, consistent with the weathering hypothesis and potentially linked to chronic stress from racial discrimination, though they paradoxically received fewer endometriosis and gynecological pain diagnoses, suggesting possible racial bias in clinical ascertainment. Socioeconomic factors including insurance status (particularly lack of coverage among Hispanic patients and Medicaid dependence among Black patients) and reproductive history (parity and family-building intentions) substantially explained symptom severity differences and delayed treatment initiation. While LNG-IUDs effectively reduce bleeding and pain symptoms with minimal adverse effects, their efficacy may be compromised in fibroid patients who experience higher expulsion rates, raising concerns about treatment effectiveness for the most severely affected populations. These findings highlight how intersecting barriers of access, diagnosis, and biological predisposition contribute to disparate gynecological health outcomes across ethno-racial groups.

title: Chronic Conditions and Adherence to COVID-19 Preventive Measures in Pitt County, North Carolina
summary: This study examined how chronic conditions influence adherence to COVID-19 preventive measures and vaccination intent in a North Carolina population. Individuals with chronic conditions—including hypertension, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, obesity, cancer, and asthma—demonstrated significantly higher risk for severe SARS-CoV-2 infection and showed substantially greater engagement in preventive behaviors such as mask-wearing, social distancing, and crowd avoidance. Vaccination intent increased in a dose-dependent manner, with each additional chronic condition raising the odds of intending to vaccinate by 19 percent. The findings reveal that patients with multiple chronic comorbidities were more likely to adopt protective health behaviors and express willingness to vaccinate, suggesting that disease burden and perceived vulnerability drive preventive health decision-making. These results highlight the complex relationship between chronic disease burden and health behavior compliance during pandemic conditions, with important implications for understanding how comorbid conditions shape patient responses to public health interventions.

title: Changing Etiological Spectrum of Premature Ovarian Insufficiency over the Past Decades: A Comparative Analysis of Two Cohorts from a Single Center
summary:
# Summary

This comparative analysis of premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) cases from two decades reveals a significant shift in the etiological spectrum of this disorder. The historical cohort (1978–2003) was characterized by a predominance of idiopathic cases (52.3%), whereas the contemporary cohort (2017–2024) showed markedly increased rates of iatrogenic POI (34% versus 7.6%) and autoimmune POI (18.9% versus 8.7%), with a corresponding halving of idiopathic cases. The rise in iatrogenic POI is primarily attributable to oncologic treatments, including chemotherapy with alkylating agents and radiotherapy, while endometriosis-related surgical interventions, particularly laparoscopic cystectomy for endometriomas, emerged as a major iatrogenic contributor associated with pronounced ovarian damage and reduced serum anti-Müllerian hormone levels. Additionally, autoimmune etiologies, particularly Hashimoto's thyroiditis, showed increased prevalence in contemporary cases, while genetic causes including Turner syndrome and FMR1 mutations remained relatively stable. The clinical presentation reflects both short-term consequences—infertility, menstrual disturbances, and vasomotor symptoms—and long-term morbidities including osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline, though reproductive outcomes remained limited in both cohorts.

title: Feasibility Study for the Quantification of Fullness and Discomfort in the Chest and Hypochondrium
summary: This feasibility study developed quantitative methods to objectively measure fullness and discomfort in the chest and hypochondrium (FDCH), a commonly observed symptom in functional dyspepsia patients. The researchers used algometry and three-dimensional imaging to quantify physical characteristics associated with FDCH, finding that patients with these symptoms exhibited significantly reduced pressure, depth, and stiffness measurements across multiple abdominal regions, along with decreased skin stiffness and increased tissue sensitivity. Geometric analysis revealed that FDCH patients had characteristic structural alterations including a wider sterno-costal angle, increased distance between specific anatomical landmarks, a flatter or drooping abdominal shape, and altered curvature patterns along the central meridian acupoints. Traditional Chinese medicine pattern diagnosis identified liver depression with qi stagnation and spleen deficiency as the most frequent underlying patterns in functional dyspepsia cases. The study successfully established measurable algometric and geometric biomarkers that distinguish FDCH patients from controls, providing objective tools to complement existing subjective clinical assessments of abdominal shape, stiffness, and tenderness. These quantitative measurements may improve diagnosis and monitoring of this common functional dyspepsia presentation.

title: Global burden and risk factors of peptic ulcer disease between 1990 and 2021: An analysis from the global burden of disease study 2021
summary:
# Summary

Between 1990 and 2021, peptic ulcer disease (PUD) remains a significant global health burden with substantial variation across regions and populations. Smoking, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), aspirin, and alcohol consumption emerged as the primary modifiable risk factors, with smoking identified as the predominant contributor to PUD-related disability-adjusted life years and deaths in both sexes. Helicobacter pylori infection also demonstrated a strong association with PUD development, while estrogen appears to provide protective effects by inhibiting gastrin synthesis and reducing gastric acid secretion, potentially explaining the male predominance in disease incidence, prevalence, and mortality. Effective treatments including proton pump inhibitors and anti-H. pylori therapies have contributed to declining mortality rates globally, though disease burden remains highest in lower sociodemographic index regions and specific countries such as Kiribati (highest incidence) and Cambodia (highest mortality). Age-stratified analysis revealed that PUD incidence and prevalence increase with age in both sexes, with complications such as marginal ulcers occurring in 5-36% of patients following bariatric surgery.

title: Subjective, behavioural and physiological correlates of stress in women using hormonal contraceptives
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that hormonal contraceptives distinctly modulate stress responses through diverging metabolic pathways and hormone levels, with markedly different psychological and physiological outcomes. Intrauterine device (IUD) users exhibited heightened emotional reactivity to stress, reporting greater subjective stress, negative affect, and anxiety alongside reduced positive affect both in laboratory settings and daily life, despite suppressed endogenous estradiol and progesterone levels. In contrast, oral contraceptive (OC) users showed a blunted salivary cortisol response and paradoxically reported lower acute stress and negative emotions despite elevated plasma cortisol levels, indicating dissociation between physiological and subjective stress measures. Across all participants, sex hormones—progesterone, testosterone, and estradiol—exhibited differential associations with skin conductance, socio-emotional stress, and negative affect, suggesting hormone-dependent modulation of stress physiology. These findings highlight that exogenous hormone administration significantly affects psychological well-being and stress-related outcomes, underscoring the need for further investigation into stress-related disorders and mental health among women using hormonal contraceptives.

title: Demographic and Biomedical Characteristics of an Observational Cohort With Chronic Low Back Pain: A Descriptive Analysis
summary: This observational cohort study characterizes the demographic and biomedical profiles of chronic low back pain (cLBP) patients, revealing distinct sex and age-related patterns. Females demonstrate significantly higher vulnerability to cLBP and exhibit greater prevalence of comorbid pain conditions including migraine, irritable bowel syndrome, and fibromyalgia, alongside elevated rates of anxiety, depression, and thyroid disease. Older adults with cLBP present with age-related comorbidities such as osteoarthritis, diabetes, osteoporosis, and vertebral fractures, but paradoxically report lower rates of depression, anxiety, and migraine compared to younger patients. The cohort reveals substantial comorbidity burden, with osteoarthritis (58%), joint replacement history (44%), anxiety (40%), and depression (40%) being highly prevalent, and multiple chronic overlapping pain conditions affecting a substantial proportion of patients. Treatment approaches are multimodal, with exercise routines (58%) and physical therapy being most frequently employed, complemented by pharmacotherapy including nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (43%), gabapentin, opioids, and antidepressants, as well as psychological interventions such as mindfulness and meditation. These findings highlight the complex relationship between patient demographics, particularly sex and age, and the presentation of cLBP with its associated comorbidities and treatment utilization patterns.

title: Fecundability in Association With Everyday and Lifetime Discrimination
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that discrimination experiences substantially reduce fecundability across multiple forms, including everyday discrimination, lifetime discrimination, harassment, and discrimination in medical care settings, with stronger associations observed among racial and ethnic minority participants. The mechanisms linking discrimination to reduced fertility appear multifaceted: discrimination increases psychological stress and "weathering" (cumulative allostatic load), promotes distrust in medical systems leading to delayed diagnosis and reduced healthcare access, and creates economic hardship through job loss that delays childbearing. Notably, maladaptive coping responses such as self-silencing and emotional suppression following discriminatory experiences further compromise reproductive outcomes, whereas active coping strategies offer some protective benefit. The study reveals that discrimination in healthcare specifically was the strongest lifetime discrimination subtype associated with reduced fecundability, highlighting how interpersonal racism within medical settings may directly impair fertility outcomes through delayed care and dismissal of patients' concerns.

title: Autoimmune Disease is Increased in Women With Primary Ovarian Insufficiency
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary: Women with primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) demonstrate a markedly elevated prevalence of autoimmune diseases, with approximately 25% affected across multiple conditions. Autoimmune hypothyroidism is the most common manifestation (19.8% prevalence), while other significantly increased autoimmune conditions include vitiligo (15-fold higher risk), celiac disease (7.6-fold), rheumatoid arthritis (5.7-fold), and type 1 diabetes (4.1-fold). The increased autoimmune risk in POI is largely attributable to autoimmune polyglandular syndromes types 1-4, wherein adrenal autoimmunity and thyroid autoimmunity are prominent features, and adrenal cortex antibodies specifically discriminate autoimmune POI from other POI etiologies. Mechanistically, ovarian autoimmunity involves antibodies targeting steroid-producing theca cells and other ovarian antigens such as anti-enolase and anti-corpus luteum antibodies, while ovarian vasculitis may contribute to direct ovarian damage particularly in systemic lupus erythematosus. In type 1 diabetes with POI, reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels suggest mechanisms involving both autoimmunity and potential microvascular or glycosylation-related damage. These findings establish autoimmune disease as a critical comorbidity in POI requiring systematic screening and management.

title: The alternation of neural oscillations during dual task standing in older adults with low back pain
journal: European Review of Aging and Physical Activity
summary:
# Summary

This study examined neural oscillations during postural control tasks in older adults with low back pain (LBP), revealing significant alterations in brain activity patterns compared to healthy controls. Older adults with LBP demonstrated substantially poorer postural control under dual-task conditions, manifested by larger center of pressure parameters and greater body sway, which directly contributed to higher fall rates and declined quality of life. The researchers identified that theta band power in frontal and sensorimotor regions was elevated during dual-task performance and strongly correlated with balance deficits and fall risk, while beta band power increased in LBP patients and associated with greater pain intensity perception.
