These diagnostic disparities appear to stem from multiple factors including implicit bias among healthcare providers, variations in clinical presentation across racial and ethnic groups, and differences in socioeconomic status. Black or African American patients also experience higher rates of major postoperative complications and lower utilization of minimally invasive surgical approaches, while Asian women present with more severe disease (stage III/IV) when diagnosed. The review found that racial and ethnic reporting in endometriosis literature remains inconsistent, with prospective and multicentre studies documenting demographic data more frequently than retrospective or single-centre studies, and studies from the WHO Americas region reporting race and ethnicity more consistently than those from the Western Pacific region.

title: Serum metabolic disparity between patients with lymph node tuberculosis and patients with sarcoidosis: towards differential diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

This metabolomics study identified distinct serum metabolic signatures that differentiate lymph node tuberculosis from sarcoidosis, two granulomatous diseases with overlapping clinical presentations. Using NMR spectroscopy-based metabolic profiling, TB patients demonstrated elevated levels of glutamate, pyroglutamate, acetate, and leucine alongside reduced glutamine, pyruvate, and myo-inositol, indicating activated glutaminolysis and elevated host lipid metabolism characteristic of mycobacterial infection. In contrast, sarcoidosis patients showed the opposite pattern with higher glutamine, citrate, myo-inositol, and pyruvate levels. These metabolic differences achieved high diagnostic accuracy (AUC > 0.8) for glutamate, pyroglutamate, and glutamine, providing noninvasive biomarkers to distinguish TB from sarcoidosis without relying on tissue biopsy. The findings suggest that serum metabolic profiling represents a promising clinical tool for differential diagnosis of granulomatous lung diseases, particularly relevant given the nonspecific symptomatology shared between these conditions.

title: Pain, Emotions, Interoception, and Bodily Sensations in Patients With Endometriosis Bodily Sensations in Patients With Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain through multiple biological mechanisms including inflammatory responses, neurogenic factors, peripheral and central sensitization. Patients with endometriosis experience significantly greater pain intensity and interference with daily life compared to controls, along with heightened nociceptive and hedonic sensitivities and widespread bodily pain representation. Interoceptive awareness—the perception of internal bodily signals—plays a central role in pain perception, with specific interoceptive traits showing protective or aggravating effects: reduced worrying and increased trust associate with lower pain interference and better emotional regulation, while heightened emotional awareness correlates with increased pain intensity and persistent pain. Psychological and emotional factors substantially influence endometriosis-related pain outcomes, as worrying amplifies pain perception while positive emotional traits like trust reduce affective interference. The findings demonstrate that emotional processing, interoceptive awareness, and bodily sensations are interconnected systems underlying endometriosis pain, suggesting that pain management interventions should address these psychological and interoceptive dimensions alongside biological treatments.

title: Sex‐ and gender‐responsive management of anxiety disorders: future pathways for research, education, policy and practice
summary:
# Summary

Women experience anxiety disorders at significantly higher rates than men, with sex and gender minorities experiencing even greater prevalence, driven by elevated exposure to trauma, stigmatization, domestic violence, and sexual assault, as well as cortisol dysregulation and cognitive-behavioral risk factors like repetitive negative thinking. Endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain represent important female-specific conditions with strong comorbidity to anxiety disorders, often underdiagnosed with prolonged diagnostic delays. Gonadal hormones estradiol and progesterone directly regulate anxiety disorder trajectories through their effects on serotonin signaling and progesterone's conversion to allopregnanolone, which modulates GABAergic inhibitory tone in the central nervous system. Anxiety symptoms fluctuate substantially across the menstrual cycle in multiple anxiety disorders, with perimenstrual symptom exacerbation reflecting hormone-sensitivity mechanisms, particularly pronounced during pregnancy, postnatally, and perimenopause—critical windows of heightened vulnerability. Hormonal contraceptives, widely used in clinical practice, suppress ovarian hormone synthesis and may interfere with psychotherapeutic interventions like exposure therapy, necessitating sex- and gender-responsive clinical management strategies.

title: Periodontitis associated with Porphyromonas gingivalis infection is a risk factor for infertility through uterine hypertrophy
summary:
# Summary

Periodontitis caused by *Porphyromonas gingivalis* infection significantly impairs fertility through a mechanism involving uterine hypertrophy and altered hormone receptor signaling. The pathogenic bacterium triggers an immune response that generates inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and antibody responses, leading to tissue destruction and alveolar bone resorption. Women with unexplained infertility show elevated serum IgG antibody titers against multiple periodontal pathogens and experience longer conception times compared to healthy controls. In experimental models, periodontitis-induced mice exhibited uterine enlargement, significantly reduced live births with increased fetal deaths, and elevated estrogen receptor α and progesterone receptor expression in endometrial and stromal tissues. These findings suggest that periodontal infection impairs pregnancy outcomes by disrupting normal uterine physiology and hormone-dependent reproductive signaling, establishing periodontitis as a novel infectious risk factor for infertility that warrants clinical consideration in reproductive medicine.

title: Efficacy and Safety of a Digital Tapering Intervention for Patients Prescribed Opioids After Surgery: Protocol for a Prospective Exploratory Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective exploratory cohort study investigates a digital tapering intervention (Prescriby services) to reduce persistent opioid use following surgery, addressing a critical clinical challenge where postoperative opioid treatment frequently leads to dependence and misuse. Opioids effectively treat acute postoperative pain, but prolonged use carries substantial risks: each additional week of opioid use increases misuse risk by 20%, and each refill raises it by 44%, with duration of use being the strongest predictor of opioid use disorder development. Physical tolerance develops within weeks, triggering withdrawal syndromes and dependence that can progress to opioid use disorder, particularly in women who face elevated addiction risk. Success in opioid tapering is associated with younger age (21-49 years), concurrent use of non-narcotic analgesics, and absence of comorbid substance use disorder or anxiety, while telemedicine-based interventions show promise comparable to face-to-face care. The study recognizes that untreated conditions such as endometriosis and mental illness may contribute to persistent pain and complicate tapering efforts, highlighting the importance of comprehensive pain management strategies beyond pharmacological approaches.

title: Hormonal contraceptive use is associated with reduced central serotonergic activity indexed by the loudness dependence of auditory evoked potentials
summary: This study demonstrates that hormonal contraceptive (HC) use is associated with reduced central serotonergic activity, as measured by steeper loudness dependence of auditory evoked potentials (LDAEP) compared to non-users. The mechanism underlying this finding relates to opposing effects of estrogen and progestin components: estrogen promotes serotonergic tone by increasing serotonin synthesis, upregulating serotonin receptors, and reducing monoamine oxidase-A activity, while progestins reduce serotonergic signaling through decreased receptor binding and elevated MAO-A activity. Since the serotonergic system plays a central role in emotional regulation and depression pathophysiology, the progestin-dominant hormonal milieu created by HC use may explain individual variability in mood disturbances and depressive symptoms observed in HC users. These findings provide neurobiological evidence for how exogenous sex hormones modulate brain function and clarify why women using hormonal contraceptives experience variable psychiatric outcomes, with potential implications for understanding endometriosis-related mood comorbidities in this population.

title: Decoding uterine (dys)function in fibroids through multimodal assessment of functional determinants: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Uterine Fibroids and Functional Determinants: Key Findings

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that women with uterine fibroids exhibit multiple altered functional determinants compared to controls, including increased uterine perfusion, elevated myometrial stiffness, and impaired contractility. Doppler ultrasound studies show that fibroids are characterized by lower uterine artery pulsatility and resistance indices but higher blood flow velocity, reflecting enhanced vascularization driven by the angiogenic activity within fibroid tissue. Elastography measurements confirm increased myometrial stiffness through elevated elastic modulus and shear wave velocity. These structural and vascular changes arise from extracellular matrix accumulation and profibrotic signaling, which activate mechanosensitive pathways and alter hormone responsiveness, ultimately leading to myometrial remodeling that disrupts normal uterine peristalsis and contractile patterns. Abnormal peristalsis is particularly evident in symptomatic fibroids and is mechanistically linked to dysmenorrhea through transient myometrial ischemia, as well as to impaired embryo implantation during fertility treatment. The findings underscore that uterine dysfunction in fibroids results from convergent mechanical and hormonal factors that disrupt junctional zone endometrium-driven peristalsis, with severe symptoms correlating with greater decrements in uterine artery pulsatility.

title: Impact of visceral pain on migraine symptoms in comorbid patients: a retrospective observational study
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: This retrospective study demonstrates that visceral pain comorbidities—including endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, and painful bladder syndrome—significantly worsen migraine outcomes through mechanisms involving central sensitization and somatic hyperalgesia. Patients with comorbid migraine and visceral pain experience higher frequency and intensity of migraine attacks, increased medication consumption, and markedly reduced pain thresholds across multiple somatic tissues compared to migraine-only patients. The study reveals a bidirectional therapeutic relationship: effective treatment of the underlying visceral condition (hormonal or laser therapy for endometriosis, dietary intervention for IBS, or bladder instillations for painful bladder syndrome) leads to substantial improvements in migraine frequency, intensity, and pain thresholds, while migraine prophylaxis with amitriptyline similarly improves both migraine parameters and sensory sensitization. These findings indicate that visceral pain episodes modulate migraine pain expression and somatic tissue sensitivity, suggesting a shared pathophysiological mechanism. The authors conclude that management of visceral pain should be integrated into the therapeutic regimen for patients with comorbid migraine and visceral conditions to achieve optimal clinical outcomes.

title: Weight Change and Accumulation of Chronic Conditions in Women During Reproductive Years
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal study examined weight changes and the development of multiple chronic conditions in women during their reproductive years. Higher BMI and progressive weight gain were strongly associated with multimorbidity, with women developing multiple conditions showing accelerated weight gain (0.75 kg/year) even before their first diagnosis compared to 0.50 kg/year in those remaining disease-free. Obesity was specifically linked to increased risks of endometriosis, uterine fibroids, menstrual irregularity, and reduced fertility. Weight gain following diagnoses of poor mental health, musculoskeletal disorders, and asthma was documented, while notably cancer diagnosis was associated with weight loss. The study revealed that even modest weight gains of 5 kg or more significantly increased risks of type-2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and obesity-related cancers, establishing a dose-response relationship between weight accumulation and chronic disease burden.

title: Young Age at Hysterectomy and Elevated Hypertension Risk: A Combined Observational and Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

This combined observational and Mendelian randomization study demonstrates a causal relationship between hysterectomy and elevated hypertension risk, with younger age at hysterectomy conferring particularly high cardiovascular risk. Mendelian randomization analysis confirmed that genetically predicted hysterectomy status causally increases both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, supporting a biological mechanism rather than mere association. The underlying pathophysiology likely involves estrogen deficiency following hysterectomy, as estrogen normally promotes vasodilation and inhibits vascular smooth muscle proliferation; loss of ovarian function through oophorectomy or surgical disruption of ovarian blood supply further accelerates this hormonal decline. Post-hysterectomy patients demonstrated unfavorable metabolic and cardiovascular profiles, including elevated obesity, smoking prevalence, diabetes, cholesterol, triglycerides, and renal markers compared to non-hysterectomy controls. Resulting hypertension substantially increases downstream cardiovascular complications including stroke, ischemic heart disease, and chronic kidney disease, with additional complications from hysterectomy itself including genitourinary/gastrointestinal damage and ovarian hypoplasia. These findings suggest that women undergoing hysterectomy, particularly at younger ages for benign indications such as endometriosis, menorrhagia, and fibroids, warrant enhanced cardiovascular monitoring and preventive interventions.

title: Sex-specific involvement of calcitonin gene–related peptide signaling for pain in experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates sex-specific mechanisms underlying neuropathic pain in experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), a multiple sclerosis model, with implications for female-specific pain conditions including endometriosis. Female mice with established EAE exhibited enhanced neuronal plasticity characterized by increased peptidergic nociceptor outgrowth, elevated calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) expression in the dorsal root ganglia and deep dorsal horn, and upregulated regeneration-associated proteins, whereas males showed damage-associated protein expression and decreased neuronal branching. CGRP signaling amplifies pain by sensitizing second-order neurons, and blocking this pathway with the CGRP receptor antagonist CGRP8-37 effectively reversed established spontaneous pain and reduced neuronal activation in females. These findings reveal that peptidergic nociceptor plasticity and CGRP-mediated signaling represent viable therapeutic targets for neuropathic pain in female patients, particularly those with autoimmune conditions and related chronic pain disorders like endometriosis where CGRP modulation has proven clinically effective.

title: Work productivity by diseases diagnosed among workers: a study using large-scale claims data and survey data of workers in Japan
summary:
# Summary

This large-scale Japanese workers' study examined how various diagnosed conditions affect workplace productivity using insurance claims and survey data. Psychiatric and mental behavioral disorders emerged as major drivers of work productivity impairment, with depression, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder showing particularly high associations with lost productivity. Additionally, neurological conditions including migraines, tension headaches, epilepsy, and insomnia, along with menstrual disorders and dysmenorrhea in women, significantly impaired work performance through both absenteeism and presenteeism. Notably, conditions causing high productivity loss were often underaddressed by healthcare systems, with psychiatric disorders, headaches, and menstrual problems showing characteristically low physician consultation rates despite their substantial workplace impact. Mental and behavioral disorders also ranked among the highest contributors to overall disease burden and healthcare costs, alongside musculoskeletal and cardiovascular diseases, underscoring the broader societal impact of inadequately managed mental health conditions in the working population.

title: The Burden of Poor Reproductive Health in England: Results From a Cross‐Sectional Survey
summary: This cross-sectional survey examined the burden of reproductive health conditions in England across 13 indicators organized into three domains: reproductive morbidities, menstrual health, and pregnancy-related outcomes. Endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome emerged as the most common reproductive morbidities (8.8% and 10.5% respectively), while heavy menstrual bleeding affected 40% of participants, with severe period pain reported by 18.7%. Significant health disparities were identified, with Black, Black British, Caribbean, and African women experiencing substantially higher odds of reproductive morbidity, heavy or severely painful periods, and pregnancy-related adverse experiences compared to White participants. Financial insecurity was strongly associated with poor reproductive health outcomes across multiple domains, including reproductive morbidities, menstrual symptoms, and unplanned pregnancy. The findings also revealed distinct age patterns, with menstrual-related issues peaking in younger women and women over 40, while pregnancy-related complications and infertility treatment-seeking peaked in women in their thirties. These results underscore substantial inequalities in reproductive health burden driven by ethnicity and socioeconomic factors.

title: Sex- and age-specific multimorbidity networks in middle-aged inpatients: a network-based comparative study between China and the United Kingdom
summary:
# Summary

This comparative network analysis of middle-aged inpatients in China and the United Kingdom reveals distinct sex- and age-specific multimorbidity patterns. Males demonstrated higher overall multimorbidity prevalence and greater complexity of disease patterns in both countries, with circulatory, genitourinary, and endocrine/metabolic diseases serving as key hub diseases. Females exhibited distinct genitourinary and neoplasm-associated patterns, with endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disorders emerging as significant female-specific hub diseases in the UK. Age substantially increased multimorbidity prevalence and disease count across both sexes, though the disease patterns shifted with aging—males developed more circulatory and metabolic complications, while females experienced increased nervous system and mental/behavioral conditions. China demonstrated significantly higher multimorbidity complexity compared to the United Kingdom, attributable to unequal medical resource allocation, limited health awareness, and lifestyle factors including high-fat diets, insufficient exercise, and air pollution. The UK's National Health Service infrastructure appears to reduce multimorbidity risk through early detection and disease management strategies.

title: Trends in global and national infertility and factors associated with primary infertile couples in recent middle-aged Chinese
summary: This epidemiological study examines the prevalence and risk factors for primary infertility among middle-aged Chinese couples, finding that approximately 1.7% of this population experiences infertility with notably higher rates in females compared to males. The research identifies psychological factors, particularly anxiety and stress, as significant contributors to infertility risk, with evidence suggesting that anxiety negatively impacts semen quality and reproductive outcomes. Beyond psychological factors, infertile couples demonstrate characteristic lifestyle patterns including reduced physical activity, excessive alcohol consumption, and younger age at presentation compared to fertile couples. Geographically, infertility burden is concentrated in high-middle and middle sociodemographic index countries, with China showing stable yet substantial prevalence rates over recent decades. The study emphasizes that the reproductive population in China faces compounded challenges from high occupational stress and workload demands, which may amplify psychological impacts on fertility. Overall, the findings suggest that addressing modifiable lifestyle factors and psychological health may be important interventions for improving fertility outcomes in middle-aged Chinese populations.

title: European Consensus on Functional Bloating and Abdominal Distension—An ESNM/UEG Recommendations for Clinical Management
summary:
# Summary

This European consensus document addresses functional bloating and abdominal distension, gastrointestinal symptoms affecting approximately 3.5% of the global population with higher prevalence in women, older adults, and patients with disorders of gut-brain interaction. The pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving visceral hypersensitivity, abdomino-phrenic dyssynergia, intestinal dysmotility, dysbiosis, and aberrant gas production by the gut microbiota, which frequently co-occur with organic diseases such as celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, and small intestinal bacterial overgrowth. Management is comprehensive and includes dietary interventions (low FODMAP diet, lactose limitation), pharmacological agents (antispasmodics, probiotics, antibiotics, laxatives, antidepressants, and anxiolytics), and psychologically-based therapies such as cognitive behavioral therapy and hypnotherapy. The document emphasizes that in patients with disorders of gut-brain interaction, visible abdominal distension results from coordinated contraction of the diaphragm and anterior abdominal wall relaxation rather than simply excessive intraluminal gas, highlighting the importance of a personalized, multimodal treatment approach for optimal clinical outcomes.

title: Exploring Young People’s Views on Pharmaceutical Care for Long-Term Illnesses in Primary Care Settings
summary: This study examined pharmaceutical care perspectives among young people with long-term illnesses in primary care, finding that 15.7% of participants (33/210) had chronic conditions, predominantly asthma, endocrine disorders including diabetes, and endometriosis affecting the female genital system. Young people demonstrated high rates of medication non-adherence, particularly those with comorbid ADHD or depression, and substantially underutilized pharmacists as a medicines information resource despite pharmacists' established role in medication management. The studied population experienced significant daily symptom burden, with approximately one-third reporting daily or more frequent symptoms, and these conditions substantially impacted their quality of life, with over half reporting effects on peer relationships and nearly two-thirds missing university days due to illness. While most young people primarily obtained health information from doctors rather than pharmacists, the majority (72.7%) supported using digital media for pharmaceutical education. These findings highlight a critical care gap: young people with long-term illnesses require enhanced pharmaceutical support and readily accessible, preferred information sources to improve medication adherence and health outcomes in primary care settings.

title: Opinions on Ketogenic Diets Among Students and Academic Teachers at the University of Pécs, Hungary: A Cross-Sectional Survey
summary:
# Summary

This survey examined knowledge and opinions regarding ketogenic diets among students and faculty at the University of Pécs, Hungary. The ketogenic diet demonstrated high-confidence associations with treating obesity and diabetes, while also showing beneficial relationships with body weight reduction, improved blood lipids, and better glycemic parameters through the achievement of nutritional ketosis. Knowledge acquisition patterns revealed that students completing nutrition-related courses and those relying on PubMed as an information source demonstrated significantly higher knowledge scores, whereas staff members currently adhering to a ketogenic diet paradoxically exhibited lower knowledge scores. Disciplinary background substantially influenced ketogenic diet knowledge, with Medical School, Engineering and Information Technology, and Music and Visual Arts students achieving significantly higher knowledge scores compared to students from Law and Humanities and Social Sciences programs.

title: Changes in anti-mullerian hormone levels after recovery from functional hypothalamic amenorrhea: a retrospective cohort study about women with and without polycystic ovarian morphology
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined how anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) levels and ovarian morphology change after menstrual recovery in women with functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (FHA), comparing those with and without polycystic ovarian morphology (PCOM). The researchers found that nearly half of FHA patients exhibit PCOM at baseline, with the FHA-PCOM subgroup showing elevated prolactin, AMH, testosterone, and insulin resistance along with reduced FSH and sex hormone binding globulin. The key mechanism linking these conditions involves relative FSH deficiency from hypothalamic dysfunction, which leads to reduced AMH production and polycystic ovarian features. Notably, after menstrual recovery, FHA-PCOM patients demonstrated significant decreases in AMH levels and resolution of PCOM in approximately 52% of cases, indicating that the polycystic morphology represents a reversible state of ovarian hyperactivation rather than true PCOS. Psychological stress emerged as a major causative factor in FHA-PCOM patients, while low-but-normal FSH levels appear sufficient for follicular recruitment but insufficient for complete follicular growth and ovulation. These findings support the hypothesis that PCOM in FHA represents a functional, reversible condition distinct from primary PCOS, and clinicians should avoid prematurely diagnosing low ovarian reserve in FHA patients based solely on reduced AMH levels.

title: Physical activity levels and self-perception among patients living with chronic conditions in France: A population-based cross-sectional study using the ComPaRe cohort
journal: The European Journal of General Practice
summary: This cross-sectional study of French patients with chronic conditions reveals a critical paradox: while physical activity substantially reduces mortality and improves both mental health and disease outcomes, many patients significantly misperceive their actual activity levels, undermining motivation for behavioral change. The most prevalent conditions in the study population were hypertension, chronic low back pain, and diabetes, conditions that would particularly benefit from increased physical activity engagement. Notably, patients with chronic diseases demonstrated a higher tendency toward underestimation rather than overestimation of their activity levels, contrary to the pattern observed in healthier populations who tend to overestimate their efforts. The study identifies that personalized physical activity prescriptions incorporating feedback mechanisms, goal setting, and shared decision-making emerged as the most effective strategies for sustained patient engagement, while wearable technologies show promise for improving the accuracy of activity assessment. COVID-19 lockdowns significantly exacerbated sedentary behavior, highlighting the vulnerability of chronic disease patients to external disruptions in physical activity routines. These findings underscore that addressing perception-behavior gaps through targeted, individualized interventions is essential for improving physical activity adherence and health outcomes in chronic disease populations.

title: Identification of Risk Factors in Patients with Recurrent Cystitis May Improve Individualized Management
summary:
# Summary: Risk Factors in Recurrent Cystitis

This study identified multiple independent predictors of recurrent cystitis (RC) that can guide personalized clinical management. Chronic constipation, severe symptom impact on daily activities, multiple uropathogens, and trace proteinuria each independently predicted RC development, with multiple uropathogens showing the strongest association (OR 12.53). RC patients exhibited distinct clinical and microbiological profiles, including higher rates of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, notably Enterococcus species in polymicrobial infections, alongside greater psychological burden manifested as extreme anxiety and depression. Conversely, acute sporadic cystitis was characterized by presence of pyuria, absence of daily activity limitations, and flank pain, indicating distinct pathophysiological mechanisms. The authors suggest that systematic assessment of these risk factors—particularly gastrointestinal comorbidities and psychological impacts alongside microbiological findings—could enable stratified treatment approaches, potentially reducing antibiotic exposure and associated resistance while improving quality of life outcomes in RC patients.

title: Genome-Wide Association Study of Daughter Pregnancy Rate in Crossbred Dairy Cows
summary: This genome-wide association study identified multiple genetic loci contributing to daughter pregnancy rate (DPR) in crossbred dairy cows, with SLX4IP, KDSR, and MC1R emerging as major effect genes. The research demonstrates that crossbred Jersey-Holstein cattle exhibit substantially higher pregnancy rates than pure Holstein breeds due to complementary additive effects and favorable allelic combinations between the two breeds. Beyond single-locus effects, the study revealed extensive epistatic interactions, particularly additive×additive effects concentrated on chromosome 13 involving genes such as PLCB1, PLCB4, PAK5, and SNAP25, along with dominance×dominance effects predominantly on chromosome 20. The genetic mechanism underlying DPR heterosis involves both within-locus allelic interactions that confer homozygous advantage and multilocus genotype×genotype interactions, with crossbreeding activating new additive effects undetectable in pure breeds. These findings indicate that the fertility advantage of crossbred dairy cows results from a complex polygenic architecture combining additive effects, epistatic interactions, and breed-specific allelic combinations rather than single genetic variants.

title: Disparities in women’s health and clinical considerations from a translational science perspective: A narrative review and framework for future directions
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines sex and gender disparities in women's health outcomes from a translational science perspective. Women experience significantly greater health burden, including 25% more years in poor health than men, with endometriosis contributing approximately 5% of this burden alongside other sex-specific conditions. The article identifies multiple interconnected pathways linking mental health disorders—which disproportionately affect women including depression, anxiety, and eating disorders—to chronic physical conditions through shared neurobiological mechanisms involving neuroinflammation and systemic inflammatory markers. Key conditions affecting women more severely include autoimmune diseases (multiple sclerosis, systemic lupus erythematosus, fibromyalgia), cardiovascular disease with worse outcomes, Alzheimer's disease, and post-acute COVID-19 sequelae. Importantly, gender bias in healthcare settings contributes to differential treatment, with women's pain reports taken less seriously than men's, leading to disparities in diagnosis and treatment. The review highlights depression as a modifiable risk factor for dementia and emphasizes that early life trauma and psychiatric disorders significantly increase long-term neurocognitive complications, underscoring the need for integrated, sex-informed clinical approaches.

title: Heavy or healthy? Assessing menstrual bleeding and bleeding tendency in Dutch adolescents
journal: Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis
summary: This study examined the relationship between menstrual bleeding patterns and underlying bleeding disorders in Dutch adolescents aged 12-21 years. Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB), defined as a PBAC score ≥150 corresponding to blood loss >80 mL per cycle, affected approximately 20% of adolescents and was associated with bleeding disorders in 10-62% of cases. The research found a strong association between objective measures of heavy bleeding (PBAC score) and subjective assessments (self-BAT scores), with those perceiving abnormal menstrual loss having a sixfold higher chance of severe bleeding on clinical evaluation. Notably, over 60% of adolescents with self-reported normal menstrual bleeding actually met objective criteria for HMB, highlighting a significant perception gap between patients' subjective experience and measured blood loss. Contraceptive use, including hormonal oral contraceptives, intrauterine devices, and tranexamic acid, effectively reduced menstrual bleeding symptoms. The study identified endometriosis, adenomyosis, and polycystic ovarian syndrome as associated gynecological conditions in adolescents with menstrual complaints, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive clinical assessment in this population.

title: Exploring the Evolution of Leiomyomatosis Peritonealis Disseminata: A Longitudinal Study on Malignant Transformation and Potential Biomarkers
summary: Leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata (LPD) is a rare smooth muscle tumor entity characterized by multiple intra-abdominal and pelvic nodules that predominantly affects childbearing-age females and carries an uncertain but potentially serious biological behavior. The longitudinal analysis identified grade-based risk stratification, with G2 tumors presenting at more advanced stages (larger size, older patient age, higher symptom rates) and G3 tumors undergoing frank malignant transformation, while even seemingly benign G1 lesions may progress to malignancy with extended follow-up. Loss of hormone receptor expression emerged as a key biomarker associated with malignant transformation, as hormone receptors were present in only 24.4% of cases overall. Malignant progression leads to aggressive disease courses with hematogenous dissemination, including liver and lung metastases. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) levels were identified as a potentially useful biomarker for predicting the development of metastatic disease. These findings underscore the importance of long-term surveillance in LPD patients and highlight hormone receptor status and ctDNA as valuable molecular markers for risk stratification and malignant transformation prediction.

title: Minimal Clinically Important Changes of Patient-reported Outcome Measures for Acute Postsurgical Pain
summary: This study establishes clinically meaningful thresholds for assessing postsurgical pain outcomes across diverse surgical populations, including endometriosis procedures. Patient-reported outcome measures for pain intensity demonstrate minimal clinically important difference (MCID) values ranging from 1.2 for pain at rest and average pain to 1.6 for pain during physiotherapy, while physical function improvements range from 1.5 to 1.6 depending on the specific domain measured. A critical finding is that baseline pain severity substantially influences MCID estimates, with patients starting from mild baseline pain requiring smaller changes (MCID of 1.0 at rest) to perceive meaningful improvement, whereas those with severe baseline pain require larger improvements (MCID of 2.1 at rest). Endometriosis surgical patients showed relatively high rates of persistent preoperative pain (44%) and some opioid use (8%), underscoring the clinical complexity of this population. These baseline-adjusted MCID values provide clinicians with more nuanced decision-making tools that account for individual patient starting points when evaluating treatment effectiveness. The findings emphasize that statistical significance alone does not guarantee clinically relevant benefit and that baseline pain levels must be considered when interpreting postsurgical pain improvements.

title: Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a Model for Reprogramming of Eukaryotic Cells: Implications for the Study of the Relationship Between Metabolism and Inflammation in Chronic Disease
summary:
# Summary

This study uses *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* as a model to demonstrate that metabolic reprogramming through fasting induces mitochondrial changes that enhance coupling efficiency and metabolic flexibility. Fasted yeast progeny maintained higher respiratory control ratios and pyruvate dehydrogenase complex activity while displaying lower oxygen consumption rates, indicating a shift toward tighter mitochondrial coupling compared to high-glucose-exposed yeast that exhibited reduced pyruvate dehydrogenase activity and a fermentation bias. Lipopolysaccharide exposure triggered divergent immune responses in the two populations: it decreased coupling efficiency in fasted progeny while increasing reserve capacity in high-glucose progeny, suggesting that metabolic programming determines cellular responses to inflammatory signals. The authors propose that this metabolic reprogramming mechanism has implications for understanding chronic inflammatory diseases, including endometriosis, where excessive fermentation bias associates with epithelial cell proliferation and proinflammatory states analogous to M1 macrophage metabolism. These findings establish that dietary and metabolic interventions can reprogram coupling efficiency regulation at the mitochondrial level, potentially offering therapeutic targets for metabolic-inflammatory comorbidities.

title: Behçet’s syndrome within the spectrum of systemic vasculitides
summary: Behçet's syndrome is a complex relapsing inflammatory vasculitis driven by exaggerated innate immune responses, particularly involving neutrophils, the IL-23–IL-17 axis, and CD8+ T cells. The disease is strongly associated with HLA-B51, accounting for 32–52% of cases, and shares autoinflammatory characteristics with familial Mediterranean fever, responding to colchicine and immunosuppressive therapies. Pathologically, neutrophil-dominant infiltration produces reactive oxygen species and neutrophil extracellular traps that promote a prothrombotic state through platelet activation and endothelial dysfunction, leading to the hallmark manifestation of vascular inflammation-induced thrombosis, predominantly affecting veins including lower extremity deep venous thrombosis and superior/inferior vena cava thrombosis. Clinically, Behçet's syndrome presents with oral ulcers (often the first symptom), genital ulcers, skin lesions, arthritis affecting knees and ankles, and severe ocular involvement including bilateral panuveitis and retinal vasculitis that can cause legal blindness in 23% of patients. The disease shows marked geographic variation with highest prevalence along the historic Silk Road and particularly in Turkey (421 per 100,000), affects young males disproportionately, and can involve gastrointestinal, neurological, and pulmonary systems. Anti-TNF biologic agents have significantly improved visual outcomes, representing major advances in treatment alongside traditional glucocorticoids and immunosuppressive therapies.

title: Multimodal Psychophysiological Assessment Reveals Gastric but Not Cardiac Interoception Deficits in Disorders of Gut‐Brain Interaction
summary:
# Gastric Interoception Deficits in Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction

Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction (DGBI) are characterized by visceral hypersensitivity and interoceptive dysfunction stemming from impaired gut-brain axis communication involving the central, enteric, and autonomic nervous systems. This multimodal psychophysiological study revealed that patients with DGBI exhibit significantly reduced gastric interoception compared to healthy controls, demonstrating a modality-specific impairment since cardiac interoception remained normal. The gastric interoceptive deficit was evidenced by patients requiring substantially more water to achieve fullness sensation and showed robust correlation with the "Trusting" subscale of interoceptive awareness measures, suggesting impaired confidence in body signals. Gastric dysfunction, measured by electrogastrography peak frequency, correlated with heightened affective symptom distress, while patients consistently reported elevated negative affect, greater visceral sensitivity concerns, and dysfunctional thoughts about bodily symptoms during the water-load test. These findings demonstrate that impaired gastric interoception is specifically associated with dysregulated physiological and affective responses in DGBI, highlighting the importance of visceral-specific mechanisms in the pathophysiology of gut-brain disorders and their comorbid psychological manifestations including anxiety and depression.

title: Living Gluten-Free in Romania: A National Cross-Sectional Study of Dietary Adherence in Clinically Diagnosed and Self-Reported Cases
summary:
# Summary

This Romanian cross-sectional study examines gluten-related disorders (celiac disease, non-celiac gluten sensitivity, and wheat allergy) and dietary adherence to gluten-free diets. Celiac disease is triggered by gluten exposure in genetically predisposed individuals carrying HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 haplotypes, causing characteristic small intestinal damage including villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and inflammation that leads to nutrient malabsorption and multiple extraintestinal manifestations such as iron deficiency anemia, autoimmune thyroiditis, and cardiovascular disease. A gluten-free diet remains the only effective therapeutic intervention for both celiac disease and non-celiac gluten sensitivity. The study found that medically confirmed diagnosis, longer time since diagnosis, greater perceived importance of dietary adherence, and consistent food label reading predict better gluten-free diet compliance, while cost of specialized foods and social activity restrictions act as significant barriers. Notably, patients with clinically confirmed celiac disease demonstrated significantly higher dietary adherence than self-diagnosed non-celiac gluten sensitivity patients, highlighting the critical role of medical validation in treatment outcomes.

title: Dietary and Lifestyle Patterns and Their Associations with Cardiovascular and Inflammatory Biomarkers in Vegans, Vegetarians, Pescatarians, and Omnivores: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

Plant-based diets, particularly vegan diets combined with healthy lifestyle behaviors, are associated with reduced cardiometabolic risk through multiple mechanisms including favorable body composition, lower visceral adiposity, and improved lipid profiles compared to omnivorous diets. The anti-inflammatory benefits of plant-based diets stem from their high content of polyphenols and antioxidants, which modulate inflammatory pathways and decrease circulating levels of CRP and IL-6, markers strongly correlated with adiposity measures and systemic inflammation. In contrast, diets high in saturated fats and low in dietary fiber promote pro-inflammatory responses and elevated cardiovascular biomarkers. Since chronic low-grade inflammation is implicated in the pathogenesis of multiple non-communicable diseases including endometriosis and rheumatoid arthritis, and can progress to serious conditions such as neuroinflammation and cognitive decline, dietary intervention through increased plant-based food consumption may offer therapeutic potential for inflammation-related comorbidities. However, vegans with suboptimal lifestyle behaviors show elevated LDL-C, non-HDL-C, and homocysteine levels, indicating that diet quality and behavioral factors are critical modifiers of cardiovascular outcomes. These findings underscore that minimizing visceral adiposity and maintaining healthy body weight through appropriate dietary and lifestyle patterns are essential for reducing both cardiovascular and inflammatory risk regardless of dietary philosophy.

title: Acceptance of disease as a mediator between social support and quality of life in women with Hashimoto’s disease—a pilot study
summary: This pilot study examined how women with Hashimoto's disease achieve quality of life outcomes, identifying disease acceptance as a critical mediator between social support and health-related quality of life. Hashimoto's disease, an autoimmune thyroid condition characterized by elevated anti-thyroid peroxidase and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies that predominantly affects women, frequently leads to hypothyroidism and co-occurs with endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and other chronic conditions, while also increasing risk for depression and anxiety disorders. The research revealed that social support from family, friends, and healthcare providers—delivered through emotional, informational, evaluative, and instrumental channels—directly improves well-being but operates primarily through patients' psychological acceptance of their disease. Elevated thyroid antibody levels was associated with lower quality of life, suggesting that immune activation itself contributes to disease burden independent of hormonal status. The findings underscore that psychological interventions targeting disease acceptance, combined with strengthened social support networks, represent important therapeutic targets beyond standard levothyroxine treatment for improving outcomes in women with Hashimoto's disease.

title: A Community-Engaged Approach for Assessment of Cortisol Dynamic Range and Multilevel Chronic Stress in African Americans: FAITH! Heart Health+ Ancillary Study
summary: This study examined cortisol dynamic range (CDR) as a biomarker of chronic stress in African American women, revealing that flattened diurnal cortisol patterns—characterized by reduced variability between morning and evening levels—correlate with elevated perceived stress, anxiety, and adoption of the "Superwoman Schema," a coping mechanism involving emotional suppression and high-effort resilience despite adversity. Lower CDR was significantly associated with greater distance from George Floyd Square, suggesting that exposure to community trauma and structural racism contributes to physiological dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. The research demonstrates that cumulative stressors including interpersonal and structural racism, poor sleep quality, and John Henryism-type coping strategies all correlate with suppressed cortisol responsiveness, while childhood adversities similarly predict flattened cortisol ranges and downstream cognitive impairment. This community-engaged investigation highlights how multilevel chronic stress—from individual emotional regulation failures to neighborhood-level racial trauma—manifests as measurable HPA axis dysfunction in African American women, with potential transgenerational implications for health outcomes.

title: Sex-Specific Genetic Architecture and Comorbidities of Alcohol Use Behaviors
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals striking sex-specific differences in alcohol use behaviors and their genetic architecture. Males demonstrate higher propensity for heavy drinking and problematic alcohol use, with genetic variants in ADH1B, KLB, and FTO showing male-specific associations with beer consumption, while females exhibit rapid progression to alcohol dependence despite lower overall consumption levels, with the IZUMO1 locus showing female-specific effects on drinking frequency and quantity. The comorbidity profiles diverge dramatically by sex: males show stronger genetic correlations with externalizing traits including other substance use disorders, ADHD, and antisocial behavior, whereas females present predominantly internalizing psychopathology such as depression and self-harm alongside disproportionate medical complications including liver disease and musculoskeletal conditions even at lower alcohol exposure levels. Biological factors including hormones, body composition, and metabolism contribute to these sex-specific trajectories. These findings underscore fundamental differences in how genetic susceptibility and environmental factors interact to shape alcohol use trajectories and associated health outcomes between sexes, with important implications for sex-tailored prevention and treatment strategies.

title: Prothrombotic fibrin clot properties are associated with lower live births rate in women with obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome: a cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This cohort study demonstrates that prothrombotic fibrin clot properties significantly impair reproductive outcomes in women with obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome (OAPS). The pathogenic mechanism involves anti-ß2-glycoprotein I antibodies triggering defective spiral arterial remodeling, which subsequently impairs trophoblast proliferation and disrupts early placentation. Women with OAPS exhibit reduced fibrin clot permeability (lower Ks values) and prolonged clot lysis times due to elevated fibrinolytic inhibitors—plasminogen activator inhibitor type 1 and thrombin activatable fibrinolysis inhibitor—creating a prothrombotic state associated with lower live birth rates. Notably, women who achieved live births showed higher baseline fibrin clot permeability and lower levels of antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG, and antiphosphatidylserine/prothrombin IgG), suggesting that fibrin clot phenotype serves as a predictor of pregnancy success. Standard treatment with low-molecular-weight heparin and low-dose aspirin is employed, with emerging evidence supporting hydroxychloroquine as an adjunctive therapy to improve obstetric outcomes in OAPS patients.

title: The Relationships Between MASLD, Extrahepatic Multimorbidity, and All-Cause Mortality in the UK Biobank Cohort
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary: Metabolic dysfunction-associated fatty liver disease (MASLD) is strongly associated with extensive multimorbidity and increased all-cause mortality in this UK Biobank cohort study. Individuals with MASLD demonstrated a significantly higher prevalence of multimorbidity (21.3% versus 14.4%) and exhibited elevated mortality risk (HR 1.16), with particular associations across metabolic, cardiovascular, renal, respiratory, and mental health domains. The analysis revealed that MASLD patients were more likely to have 32 of 47 assessed long-term conditions, with notably high associations for gout, heart failure, post-traumatic stress disorder, and osteoarthritis, while substance use disorders, osteoporosis, and eating disorders were paradoxically less common. Each additional comorbidity in MASLD patients increased mortality risk by approximately 30–38%, with cardiovascular and neurological conditions demonstrating the strongest independent associations with mortality, including Parkinson disease (HR 6.09) and hematological cancers (HR 2.99). Sex-stratified analysis demonstrated that females with MASLD had higher odds of stroke, ischemic heart disease, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease compared to males, suggesting important gender-specific disease patterns. These findings underscore MASLD as a significant driver of complex multimorbidity with substantial mortality implications that extend far beyond hepatic pathology.

title: Sleep health challenges among women: insomnia across the lifespan
summary:
# Summary

Women experience significantly higher rates of insomnia than men (OR = 1.58), with multiple interconnected biological and psychosocial mechanisms contributing to this sex disparity. Sex differences in neurobiology, hormonal variation, and psychological factors including anxiety and depression—conditions for which women have two to three times greater lifetime risk—are key drivers of insomnia across the female lifespan. During pregnancy, sleep deteriorates progressively from the first trimester through the third trimester, where inadequate nighttime sleep (<6 hours) is linked to clinical depression, while comorbid sleep disorders such as obstructive sleep apnea and restless leg syndrome further compromise sleep quality and maternal health outcomes. In the perimenopausal and menopausal years, declining estrogen and elevated follicle-stimulating hormone levels contribute to sleep disturbance, affecting up to 26% of women who develop chronic clinical insomnia after menopause. Notably, endometriosis is associated with disordered sleep and sleep dysfunction, and poor sleep quality in women with endometriosis correlates independently with reduced quality of life, depressive symptoms, and increased dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain burden. Treatments including cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia, estrogen replacement therapy, and complementary approaches such as yoga and acupuncture show efficacy in improving sleep outcomes across different life stages.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of pneumococcal disease among children and adolescents aged <20 years from 1990 to 2021: a predictive analysis
summary:
# Summary

Streptococcus pneumoniae causes severe invasive diseases including pneumonia, meningitis, and sepsis in children and adolescents aged less than 20 years, representing a leading cause of preventable deaths globally. The burden of pneumococcal disease is inversely correlated with sociodemographic development, with low-SDI regions experiencing the highest mortality rates, particularly among children under five years and males. Key protective factors include pneumococcal conjugate vaccination, robust healthcare systems, antimicrobial therapies, adequate nutrition, and specialized pediatric care, all of which demonstrably reduce disease severity and mortality. However, emerging challenges including antibiotic resistance, serotype replacement, vaccination gaps, and conflicts continue to sustain pneumococcal infection rates across populations. From 1990 to 2021, epidemiological improvements achieved substantial burden reduction, though this progress was substantially offset by population growth, which contributed to a 38.75% increase in absolute mortality numbers. Expansion of vaccine coverage and strengthening health systems infrastructure remain critical strategies for further reducing the global pneumococcal disease burden in vulnerable pediatric populations.

title: Differential risk of adverse drug reactions with baricitinib across age groups: integrating real-world pharmacovigilance and genetic causal inference
summary:
# Summary

Baricitinib, a JAK inhibitor used to treat rheumatoid arthritis and COVID-19, inhibits the JAK-STAT signaling pathway to reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines, but demonstrates distinct adverse drug reaction (ADR) profiles across age groups in real-world pharmacovigilance data. Elderly patients (≥66 years) show significantly higher rates of thrombotic events (deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism), pneumonia, interstitial lung disease, and malignant neoplasms, with age-related renal function decline reducing baricitinib clearance and exacerbating risk. Younger adults (18-65 years) experience elevated hepatic enzyme elevation, staphylococcal pneumonia, acute myocardial infarction, and certain malignancies including breast cancer and lung adenocarcinoma. Genetic causal inference reveals that higher JAK2 expression is protective against thrombotic events and atrial fibrillation, while elevated JAK1 expression increases atrial fibrillation risk, explaining the mechanistic basis for observed thrombotic complications. Time-to-onset analysis demonstrates that thrombotic ADRs manifest as early failures shortly after treatment initiation, whereas malignancies emerge as late-onset complications, and herpes zoster exhibits random failure patterns indicating consistent safety concerns throughout therapy. Females account for 63.4% of ADR reports, highlighting the need for sex-stratified safety monitoring in baricitinib treatment across diverse patient populations.

title: Social inequalities, psychosocial influences, and symptom development in patients with irritable bowel syndrome - a longitudinal qualitative analysis of the SOMA.SOC study
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal qualitative study from the SOMA.SOC cohort reveals that irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) development is shaped by a complex interplay of social inequalities and psychosocial factors operating within a biopsychosocial framework. Socioeconomic status, gender, and occupational circumstances significantly influence IBS outcomes, with women reporting more intense symptoms and greater psychological distress than men, while higher occupational status associates with symptom improvement. Psychosocial stressors—including occupational strain, private environment stressors, childhood trauma, and perceived medical stigma—along with psychological conditions such as stress, anxiety, and depression directly influence digestive function and IBS symptom development. The study also documents IBS comorbidities with functional somatic syndromes including endometriosis, fibromyalgia, and atopic eczema, as well as associated symptoms like fatigue, nausea, and headaches. The authors emphasize that mitigating social inequalities in IBS requires addressing both individual coping strategies and broader social determinants of health, including oppression related to socioeconomic status, gender, and migration history.

title: Exploring the role of symptom monitoring in self-management of chronic physical health conditions experienced by women: protocol for a scoping review
summary: This scoping review protocol examines how symptom monitoring supports self-management in women with chronic physical health conditions, including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and autoimmune disorders. Women experience chronic conditions at higher rates than men and face documented delays in diagnosis, quality of life impacts, and mental health consequences, with disparities particularly pronounced in conditions like low back pain, depressive disorders, and headaches. Symptom monitoring—the regular tracking of symptom severity, frequency, and duration—is positioned as a core self-management skill within established frameworks such as the Stanford Model and Chronic Care Model, enabling informed decision-making and behavioral changes. Multiple tools facilitate effective symptom tracking, including digital interventions like wearables, ecological momentary assessments, symptom diaries, and mobile applications, which can track complex symptom patterns and inform personalized treatment approaches. The review acknowledges that sex and gender factors, shaped by gender norms and health system inequities, influence how women experience and report chronic conditions. Supportive enablers including healthcare relationships, peer networks, and co-designed tools are identified as key facilitators of successful self-management outcomes.

title: Breast Imaging Findings in Women with Lipedema: A Retrospective Cross-Sectional Descriptive Study
summary:
# Summary

Lipedema, a chronic adipo-fascial disorder characterized by connective tissue dysfunction, fibrosis, and microangiopathy, demonstrates a distinctive breast phenotype that reflects systemic stromal dysregulation. Women with lipedema exhibit remarkably high prevalence of fibro-glandular parenchyma (45%), multiple diffuse cysts (42%), microcalcifications (21%), and fibroadenomas (43.5%), alongside stromal hyperplasia, hypercellularity, and extracellular matrix dysregulation on histological examination. These breast findings are consistent with a generalized fibroproliferative tendency driven by M2-mediated inflammation and myofibroblast activity, with M2 macrophages promoting angiogenesis, fluid accumulation, and extracellular matrix remodeling. Notably, lipedema frequently co-occurs with other connective tissue and systemic disorders, including endometriosis, fibromyalgia, hypermobility spectrum disorders, and uterine fibroids, suggesting a broader systemic stromal dysfunction. Additionally, all three lipedema patients who underwent breast augmentation developed clinically significant capsular contracture, a complication mediated by the same TGF-β signaling, myofibroblasts, and extracellular matrix stiffening pathways that characterize lipedema itself. These findings support the reconceptualization of lipedema as a systemic connective tissue disorder with characteristic breast imaging and histological manifestations.

title: Surface Electromyography Characteristics of Pelvic Floor Muscles in Healthy Women, Pelvic Floor Dyssynergia, and Urinary Incontinence: A Retrospective Comparative Study
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: This study used surface electromyography to characterize pelvic floor muscle function across healthy women, those with pelvic floor dyssynergia, and those with urinary incontinence. Pelvic floor dyssynergia was distinguished by elevated muscle activity during tonic contractions, impaired post-contraction relaxation, paradoxical muscle activity, and reduced efficiency of rapid contractions relative to maximum voluntary contraction capacity. In contrast, urinary incontinence showed heterogeneous electromyographic patterns, reflecting its multifactorial etiology and distinct neuromuscular characteristics including reduced recruitment capacity. Age emerged as a negative predictor of post-contraction relaxation and resting activity, suggesting age-related decline in pelvic floor neuromuscular control. The findings indicate that dyssynergia and incontinence represent distinct neuromuscular phenotypes requiring differentiated treatment approaches—specifically, dyssynergia benefits from relaxation-based strategies rather than strengthening exercises that may exacerbate overactivity. These electromyographic signatures provide objective markers for diagnosis and treatment selection in pelvic floor dysfunction management.

title: Demographic and socioeconomic risk factors for pain progression and recurrence in middle-aged and older adults: multistate analysis of a prospective English cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study examined how demographic and socioeconomic factors influence pain trajectories in middle-aged and older adults, with particular relevance to conditions like endometriosis that disproportionately affect women. Female sex and socioeconomic disadvantage emerged as the strongest predictors of poor pain outcomes: women were 45% more likely to experience pain recurrence, 28% less likely to achieve pain improvement, and substantially less likely to achieve remission compared with men. Education and wealth demonstrated protective effects across multiple pain outcomes, with higher education associated with 43% greater likelihood of pain improvement and 33% reduced risk of pain recurrence, while married or partnered status also predicted better pain trajectories. The study identified sex and socioeconomic disparities as fundamental drivers of differential pain progression, with the authors suggesting that mechanisms including health literacy, access to supplementary treatments and therapies, and social support networks may explain why advantaged individuals experience more favorable long-term pain outcomes. These findings underscore how structural inequalities shape clinical trajectories in chronic pain conditions and have important implications for understanding sex-specific diseases such as endometriosis.

title: Contraceptive use in lesbian and bisexual women: findings from the Australian Longitudinal Study on Women’s Health
summary:
# Summary

Hormonal contraceptives, including pills, intrauterine devices, and implants, effectively treat endometriosis while also managing polycystic ovarian syndrome, acne, and reducing gynecological cancer risk. However, contraceptive use patterns vary significantly by sexual orientation: lesbian women consistently report lower overall contraceptive use compared to bisexual and heterosexual women, though bisexual and lesbian women increasingly adopt long-acting reversible contraception (LARCs) over short-acting hormonal methods between their early twenties and late twenties. Many women use contraceptives for non-contraceptive medical purposes, including management of endometriosis and PCOS, with short-acting methods additionally providing benefits for menstrual control and acne. Notably, contraceptive use patterns are shaped by relationship status, with reduced condom use in stable heterosexual relationships and decreased overall contraceptive use in same-sex partnerships. A potential clinical concern exists that contraceptive use for symptom management may delay formal diagnosis of conditions such as endometriosis and PCOS, highlighting the need for comprehensive evaluation in women reporting relevant symptoms regardless of contraceptive status.

title: Sex and gender-related differences in neurological diseases: current challenges and recommendations for clinical practice
summary:
# Summary

This article examines sex and gender-related differences in multiple sclerosis and other neurological diseases, revealing distinct biological and clinical patterns between men and women. Multiple sclerosis shows a strong female predominance driven by X chromosome genes, including the Kdm6a locus, along with hormonal factors such as earlier menarche increasing risk and pregnancy providing protective effects through estrogen, progesterone, and hCG-mediated immune tolerance. While women with MS experience higher relapse rates and more white matter atrophy, men exhibit more aggressive disease courses with greater gray matter loss and brain atrophy, though women face clinical management challenges including delayed disease-modifying therapy initiation and higher discontinuation rates. Critical clinical considerations include careful selection of contraceptive-compatible and pregnancy-safe therapies, as several S1P receptor modulators carry teratogenic risks, while agents like interferon-β and glatiramer acetate remain safe options during pregnancy and breastfeeding. The postpartum period presents heightened reactivation risk, though exclusive breastfeeding appears protective, and women with MS using appropriate assisted reproductive technology protocols achieve live-birth rates comparable to unaffected women. These findings underscore the importance of personalized, sex-informed treatment strategies that account for reproductive planning, hormonal influences, and distinct disease phenotypes between sexes.

title: Histomorphometric Analysis of the Endometrium of Jennies (Equus asinus) and Mares (Equus caballus) in Estrus: Anatomical Differences and Possible Reproductive Implications
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This histomorphometric study reveals significant anatomical differences between donkey and horse endometrium that have important reproductive implications. Jennies (female donkeys) exhibit substantially larger uterine glands with increased luminal epithelium height, glandular lumen diameter, and greater glandular tissue percentage in the stratum compactum compared to mares, particularly in foaling animals. These species-specific endometrial structural variations appear to underlie the exacerbated inflammatory response and lower fertility outcomes observed when assisted reproductive techniques are applied to donkeys. The enlarged glandular structures in jennies promote heightened secretory activity, leading to intraluminal fluid accumulation that may trap inflammatory debris, create conditions favoring bacterial growth, and facilitate pathogenic microorganism colonization within glandular tissues. These anatomical features, combined with jennies' longer estrus cycles, longer pregnancy duration, and characteristic immune cell distribution patterns, suggest that donkey endometrial architecture creates a distinct uterine microenvironment that predisposes to compromised early embryonic development and placentation. The findings underscore the necessity of species-specific reproductive protocols rather than extrapolating equine-based assisted reproductive techniques to donkeys, particularly for conservation efforts in this endangered species.

title: Diabetes mellitus and the risk of ovarian cancer: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis of cohort and case–control studies
summary: This meta-analysis of 46 cohort and case-control studies demonstrates that diabetes mellitus is associated with a modest but statistically significant 14% increased relative risk of ovarian cancer, with type 1 diabetes showing the strongest association (46% increased risk). The mechanistic pathways linking diabetes to ovarian cancer involve multiple interconnected processes: diabetes perturbs insulin and glucose signaling, promotes chronic low-grade inflammation, and alters lipid and hormone metabolism, with these dysregulated pathways activating oncogenic cascades including PI3K–AKT–mTOR, MAPK signaling, and adiposity-related steroidogenesis. Elevated circulating glucose and insulin concentrations, combined with chronic inflammation and oxidative stress, promote cellular proliferation, impair apoptosis, and increase DNA damage, collectively facilitating carcinogenesis. The diabetes-ovarian cancer association was stronger in younger women (under 55 years), population-based studies, and Asian and Australian populations compared to America, while studies with longer follow-up periods (≥10 years) demonstrated stronger associations. Metformin may offer potential protective effects through activation of AMP-activated protein kinase and inhibition of mTOR signaling, suggesting a possible therapeutic avenue for managing cancer risk in diabetic patients.

title: Risk stratification using a nomogram model for postoperative cancer-related fatigue in elderly survivors following early-stage non-small cell lung cancer resection
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a nomogram model to stratify risk for severe cancer-related fatigue (CRF) in elderly patients following early-stage non-small cell lung cancer resection. Among 649 elderly NSCLC survivors, 32.7% experienced severe postoperative CRF, which substantially impeded functional recovery, psychological well-being, and quality of life while correlating with poor treatment adherence, elevated recurrence risk, and increased mortality. Five key risk factors were identified through LASSO and multivariable logistic regression analysis: fear of disease progression, need for caregivers, low social support, impaired activities of daily living, and poor nutrition status. The resulting nomogram demonstrated strong discrimination (AUC 0.864) with good calibration and favorable clinical utility as validated by decision curve analysis. This risk stratification tool provides clinicians with an effective means to identify elderly NSCLC survivors at highest risk for severe postoperative fatigue, enabling targeted interventions to improve patient outcomes.

title: Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome in Women: Clinical Covariates and Comorbidity Patterns
summary: This study identifies distinct clinical subgroups within chronic pelvic pain syndrome (CPPS) in women, revealing that endometriosis represents a significant comorbidity with CPPS occurring in 33.3% of CPPS patients versus 10.7% of controls. Prior pelvic surgery, bowel constipation, and trauma history emerge as major risk factors, with CPPS patients showing higher rates of dysmenorrhea, vulvodynia, and vulvar pain compared to controls. The research proposes the existence of two CPPS subtypes: an endometriosis-associated form and a neurovegetative dysregulation form characterized by autonomic dysfunction, with paracervical tenderness being a distinguishing clinical finding. Notably, commonly suspected cofactors including depression, pelvic congestion syndrome, psychosomatic factors, and various systemic conditions (diabetes, thyroid disease, hypertension) showed no significant association with CPPS, challenging prevailing etiological assumptions. Microbiome analysis revealed higher vaginal lactobacilli and lower vaginal pH in CPPS patients, suggesting a distinct microbial profile. These findings suggest that CPPS represents a heterogeneous condition requiring stratified clinical assessment and potentially differentiated treatment approaches based on endometriosis status and neurovegetative dysfunction.

title: Comorbidities in newly diagnosed multiple sclerosis patients: A population-based register study in Finland
journal: Multiple Sclerosis Journal - Experimental, Translational and Clinical
summary:
# Summary

This population-based Finnish study demonstrates that comorbidities significantly influence disease-modifying treatment (DMT) selection and persistence in newly diagnosed multiple sclerosis patients, with approximately 47% of patients having at least one comorbidity. Psychiatric disorders (22%), neurological conditions (12%), respiratory disease (7.9%), and endocrine disorders (7.4%) were most prevalent, with autoimmune comorbidities including inflammatory bowel disease, chronic iritis, and type 1 diabetes occurring at elevated rates compared to the general population. Respiratory comorbidities and older age were associated with selection of medium-efficacy over high-efficacy DMTs, while younger age and multiple prior relapses favored high-efficacy treatment initiation. Critically, multicomorbidity, psychiatric disease, autoimmune conditions, and injectable administration routes were all independently associated with lower DMT persistence and discontinuation rates, particularly for medium-efficacy treatments. These findings highlight that comorbidities act as significant barriers to treatment adherence and may necessitate personalized therapeutic approaches to optimize outcomes in MS patients.

title: Can reporting mood swings during oral contraceptive use predict peripartum depression? Results from the Swedish longitudinal cohort study Mom2B
summary:
# Summary

This Swedish longitudinal cohort study demonstrates that self-reported mood swings during oral contraceptive use predict peripartum depressive symptoms across pregnancy and postpartum periods, with women experiencing mood swings on contraceptives showing elevated depressive scores from gestational weeks 12–22 through postpartum weeks 24–35. Women with mood swings on oral contraceptives exhibit higher prevalence of endometriosis and premenstrual syndrome, conditions themselves associated with postpartum depression, suggesting a shared hormonal sensitivity phenotype. The underlying mechanism involves dysregulation of estrogen and progesterone signaling; oral contraceptives cause hormonal fluctuations and lower endogenous hormone levels that may sensitize vulnerable women, and the abrupt hormone withdrawal after delivery triggers depressive symptoms in those with established hormonal sensitivity. Peripartum depression carries substantial consequences including increased suicide risk, sleep disturbances, and adverse developmental outcomes for infants including malnutrition, reduced growth rates, and impaired cognitive development. The study identifies allopregnanolone dysregulation as a key pathophysiological pathway, with the synthetic analog brexanolone demonstrating efficacy in treating postpartum depression. These findings suggest that mood changes during hormonal contraceptive use could serve as a clinical screening tool to identify women at highest risk for peripartum depression.

title: Temporal evolution of digital health communication in Rheumatoid Arthritis: A longitudinal NLP analysis of reddit discussions (2018–2024)
summary:
# Summary

Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune condition that causes joint pain, stiffness, persistent inflammation, fatigue, and loss of mobility, frequently accompanied by psychological symptoms including anxiety, depression, and feelings of helplessness. Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), biologics, NSAIDs, and methotrexate are primary treatments, though methotrexate showed notable emotional variation in patient discussions during the COVID-19 pandemic. A longitudinal analysis of Reddit discussions from 2018–2024 revealed that RA patients expressed fear and sadness in initial posts seeking guidance, particularly newly diagnosed individuals, while commenters provided supportive messages and shared experiences conveyed in predominantly joyful and positive language. The pandemic significantly disrupted disease management, prompting increased discussions on vaccination, immune responses, drug availability, and telehealth adoption, with post-COVID conversations shifting toward chronic pain management and emerging comorbidities like carpal tunnel syndrome associated with remote work. Patients consistently prioritized understanding pain management, medication effects, and alternative symptom relief strategies including exercise, physical therapy, and anti-inflammatory diets. The analysis demonstrates how online communities capture evolving patient experiences and unmet needs, with community moderation fostering supportive dynamics that mitigate negative sentiment.

title: Pain in People With Fibromyalgia Syndrome (FMS) Undergoing or Following Surgery: A Systematic Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Fibromyalgia syndrome (FMS) is a chronic pain condition characterized by widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive dysfunction that significantly complicates the perioperative experience. Patients with FMS demonstrate heightened nociceptor hyperactivity and central amplification of pain signals, contributing to exaggerated acute postoperative pain at surgical sites and increased spontaneous widespread pain, despite preoperative mechanical hypersensitivity not predicting long-term outcomes beyond six months.
