Following surgery, FMS patients require higher opioid doses, experience slower functional recovery, and face elevated rates of postoperative complications including anemia, pneumonia, urinary tract infections, and readmissions, though paradoxically show slightly lower in-hospital mortality. Notably, surgical resolution of underlying visceral pain sources—such as endometriosis or diverticulosis—can improve FMS symptoms, with laser treatment for endometriosis and sigmoid resection for diverticulosis both reducing FMS flare frequency and enhancing pressure pain thresholds by approximately 20 percent, suggesting that addressing perpetuating visceral nociceptive contributors may reduce overall pain amplification in FMS.

title: Disentangling sex-specific mechanisms in neuropathic and nociplastic chronic pain: a review
summary:
# Summary

This review elucidates striking sex-specific mechanisms underlying chronic pain development, revealing that women experience lower pain thresholds, greater pain intensity, and heightened temporal summation compared to men, with female sex consistently predicting chronic pain trajectory. The neurobiological basis differs fundamentally between sexes: neuropathic pain in males is predominantly driven by microglial activation and release of proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, BDNF) that enhance excitatory synaptic transmission, whereas females rely more heavily on adaptive immune mechanisms involving CD4+ T-cell infiltration and release of IL-17A and interferon-γ. In nociplastic pain syndromes like fibromyalgia, which disproportionately affect women, mechanisms include hormonal fluctuations that sensitize excitatory neurotransmission and dampen descending inhibitory circuits, coupled with limbic hyperconnectivity and stress-immune dysregulation amplified by heightened hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis reactivity. Neuroimaging reveals sex-specific brain alterations, with women showing more prominent abnormalities in sensorimotor and insular regions that correlate with pain chronicity, while genome-wide studies identify largely distinct risk gene sets between sexes, enriched in the cerebellum and frontal cortex for women. These findings underscore that effective chronic pain management requires sex-informed therapeutic strategies targeting the distinct immunological and neurobiological pathways governing pain processing in women versus men.

title: Identifying potentially modifiable risk factors associated with racially disparate postoperative outcomes following benign hysterectomy
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Black patients undergoing benign hysterectomy experience significantly higher rates of major postoperative complications compared to White patients, with Black race independently associated with a twofold increased risk even after adjustment for clinical factors. This disparity is driven by a combination of nonmodifiable risk factors—including higher prevalence of obesity, diabetes, hypertension, preoperative anemia, and larger uterine weights—and disparities in access to modifiable protective factors. Black patients are less likely to undergo minimally invasive surgical approaches or be treated by high-volume surgeons, both of which substantially reduce complication rates, and more frequently experience complications from hemostatic agents and non-preferred antibiotic regimens. Several modifiable factors were independently associated with worse outcomes, including laparotomy versus minimally invasive surgery, lower surgeon volume, prolonged operative time, and use of hemostatic agents, while preferred antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin, cefoxitin, metronidazole) demonstrated protective effects. Expanding access to minimally invasive techniques, experienced high-volume surgeons, and evidence-based antibiotic regimens represents a potential strategy to mitigate these racially disparate perioperative outcomes in hysterectomy.

title: CSF estrogens’ relationships to neuroinflammatory markers and brain networks in middle-aged and older black and white women
summary: This study examined relationships between cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) estrogen levels and neuroinflammatory markers in middle-aged and older women, revealing that CSF estradiol associates with multiple inflammatory pathways including complement proteins, apoE, and RAGE, as well as with Alzheimer's disease risk genes. Estrone correlated with CSF amyloid-beta ratios (Aβ42/Aβ40), a marker of AD neuropathology, while higher CSF estrogen levels paradoxically correlated with smaller volumes in brain somatosensory and posterior-medial networks. The research identified important racial/ethnic disparities, with Black/African American women showing higher plasma estrogen levels despite lower CSF tau markers, alongside higher lifetime dementia risks and earlier menopause entry. Estrogen receptors on both neurons and glia mediate effects through nuclear transcription and membrane signaling, with early or surgical menopause associated with altered gene expression and greater AD neuropathology. Body mass index and age significantly influence CSF estrogen levels, and the relationship between BMI and plasma estrogens differs by race/ethnicity, highlighting the complex interplay between hormonal status, inflammation, and neurodegeneration risk across diverse populations.

title: Pain modulation profiles in temporomandibular disorders with migraine and fibromyalgia
journal: Journal of Oral & Facial Pain and Headache
summary: This study reveals that temporomandibular disorders (TMD), migraine, and fibromyalgia involve distinct abnormalities in endogenous pain modulation, with cumulative effects when conditions co-occur. TMD patients without comorbidities maintain relatively intact pain modulation, but those with migraine and/or fibromyalgia show progressively impaired conditioned pain modulation (pain inhibition), with the TMD plus migraine plus fibromyalgia group demonstrating virtually absent pain inhibition responses. The research demonstrates that increased temporal summation of pain (a measure of pain facilitation) correlates with disease severity, while decreased mechanical detection and pressure pain thresholds across facial and hand regions indicate widespread hypersensitivity in comorbid groups. The number of comorbid pain conditions shows a direct negative relationship with conditioned pain modulation efficacy, suggesting that central sensitization and compromised descending pain inhibitory pathways underlie the clinical pain amplification observed in multiply-affected patients. These pain modulation profiles—characterized by enhanced pain facilitation and reduced pain inhibition—appear to be a key mechanism linking TMD, migraine, and fibromyalgia as interconnected comorbid conditions rather than independent disorders.

title: Hysterectomy in women with disabilities: a systematic review
summary: This systematic review demonstrates that women with disabilities undergo hysterectomy at substantially elevated rates compared to non-disabled women, with prevalence ranging from 6.1% to 22.8% across studies, and the disparity being particularly pronounced in premenopausal women and those aged 20-44 years. Women with cognitive or multiple disabilities face especially heightened risk, while older women (60+) show diminished disparities. Women with disabilities experience higher rates of menstrual disorders, irregular bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and endometriosis—conditions that commonly trigger hysterectomy recommendations—yet face significant barriers to reproductive healthcare access including provider stigma, inadequate disability training among gynecologists, transportation challenges, and communication difficulties. Beyond the surgical risks of hysterectomy itself (infection, cardiometabolic disease, osteoporosis, dementia), concerning evidence suggests some women with disabilities undergo the procedure for non-medical reasons including contraception and menstrual hygiene management due to caregiver concerns. The review identifies critical opportunities for intervention, including prioritizing conservative management of menstrual disorders through anti-inflammatory medications and hormonal therapies, improving provider training in disability-competent reproductive care, and when surgery is necessary, recommending minimally invasive approaches that preserve fertility.

title: ‘Women deserve better’: a national mixed-methods exploration of the ‘silent’ health conditions and social issues affecting women and girls in Australia
summary: This mixed-methods study identifies multiple "silent" health conditions disproportionately affecting Australian women and girls, with endometriosis, depression, and perinatal depression emerging as conditions requiring significantly increased funding and support. The research reveals that women experience several disease burdens differently than men, including higher rates of fibromyalgia, depression, cardiovascular disease, and violence, alongside female-specific conditions such as endometriosis and menopause. Notably, women are more likely than men to experience misdiagnosis, dismissal of symptoms, and multimorbidity, while simultaneously participating less in health research and receiving inadequate care. The study demonstrates important age-related variations in health concerns, with younger women prioritizing endometriosis and perinatal depression, middle-aged women identifying peri/menopause and depression, and older women focusing on ovarian cancer and Alzheimer's disease. Geographic disparities also exist, with endometriosis concerns higher in metropolitan areas and ovarian cancer ranked higher in regional/rural regions. Overall, the findings underscore persistent gender disparities in health research, clinical recognition, and resource allocation that necessitate substantially improved responses to women's health conditions.

title: Investigating the Relationship Between Body Constitution as Defined Under Traditional Chinese Medicine and Vertigo in Women in Taiwan
summary:
# Summary

This study examined vertigo in Taiwanese women through the lens of Traditional Chinese Medicine body constitution theory, identifying significant associations between specific constitutional imbalances and vertigo risk. Yang-Xu (Yang deficiency), Yin-Xu (Yin deficiency), and Phlegm stasis body constitutions were each independently associated with increased vertigo odds, with each unit increase raising risk by approximately 3-5%. Beyond constitutional factors, age, migraine history, endometrial polyps, hormonal therapy use, and non-menstruating status emerged as substantial clinical risk factors, with women presenting endometrial polyps showing a 2.28-2.35-fold higher vertigo risk. According to TCM mechanisms, Yang deficiency impairs circulation and promotes fluid retention, Yin deficiency causes insufficient brain nourishment through deficiency-heat syndrome, and Phlegm stasis disrupts qi and blood flow—all pathways leading to vertigo symptoms. The study demonstrates that TCM-based interventions including warming Yang treatments, Yin nourishment, Phlegm dissipation, acupuncture techniques, and ear stimulation effectively address vertigo by correcting underlying constitutional imbalances. These findings suggest that vertigo in women may be substantially influenced by menstrual-hormonal factors interacting with constitutional vulnerabilities, positioning TCM constitutional assessment as a clinically relevant framework for understanding and treating this common condition.

title: Prevalence and Impact of Period and Pelvic Pain in Australian Adolescents: The PPEP Talk Schools Program
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by endometrial-like tissue growing outside the uterus, causes severe period pain and persistent pelvic pain affecting approximately one in seven Australian adult women, with substantial impacts on adolescent wellbeing and educational outcomes. The study demonstrates that period pain and persistent pelvic pain are strongly associated with school absenteeism, mental health decline, and reduced quality of life, with socioeconomic disparities evident—students from low socioeconomic status schools and regional areas experience significantly higher rates of severe pain and greater educational disruption. The PPEP Talk schools program successfully increased endometriosis awareness from 47.8% to 95.5% among students and provided pain management tools and education that enhanced self-efficacy and help-seeking behavior. Educational interventions addressing pain neuroscience and self-management strategies appear effective in reducing pain intensity and improving recovery expectations, while early management of dysmenorrhea may prevent progression to persistent pain conditions. The findings highlight endometriosis and period pain as significant public health issues with preventable consequences when addressed through evidence-based school-based education and early intervention targeting vulnerable populations.

title: Comorbidities at MS Diagnosis and Their Association With Treatment Persistence: Real‐World Clinical Data
summary:
# Summary

This real-world clinical study demonstrates that comorbidities present at multiple sclerosis diagnosis significantly impair treatment persistence, with psychiatric conditions showing the strongest associations. Patients with psychiatric comorbidity, particularly depression and anxiety, discontinue medium-efficacy injectable disease-modifying therapies (interferon-β and glatiramer acetate) at higher rates and experience earlier treatment termination compared to those without comorbidities. The mechanisms underlying this reduced persistence appear multifactorial: psychiatric patients demonstrate greater sensitivity to injection-related pain and needle fear, show higher relapse rates and greater neuroaxonal damage, and experience structural brain changes including increased lesion load and gray matter atrophy. Additionally, comorbidities including thyroid disorders, inflammatory bowel disease, and migraine are frequently present in relapsing-remitting MS patients, and collectively these comorbidities delay both MS diagnosis and initiation of disease-modifying treatments. The study highlights that adverse effects remain the primary reason for DMT discontinuation overall, but psychiatric comorbidity emerges as a critical independent predictor of treatment non-persistence that warrants clinical attention and potential intervention to improve long-term outcomes.

title: Trends in the Use and Indications for Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection Between 2005 and 2017: A State‐Wide Descriptive Cohort Analysis
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

Between 2005 and 2017, intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) use expanded dramatically in Victoria, Australia, from 60.6% to 79.5% of assisted reproduction cycles, driven by shifting indications beyond its original purpose. While ICSI effectively treats male factor infertility and addresses fertilization failure, its use increasingly extended to conditions with less robust evidence, including low oocyte count, advanced maternal age, unexplained infertility, and pre-implantation genetic testing for aneuploidy. The expansion of oocyte vitrification technology and improved fertility access for same-sex couples and single women significantly increased ICSI utilization, with donor sperm and female factors associated with poor IVF outcomes becoming major indications over the study period. Notably, empirical ICSI use—application without clear indication—rose substantially, accounting for up to 45.9% of cycles in 2010, despite emerging evidence linking ICSI to potential congenital abnormalities and adverse reproductive outcomes in non-male-factor cases. This trend toward broader ICSI application reflects evolving clinical practice patterns that may not align with evidence-based recommendations for its use.

title: Gender differences in superior hypogastric plexus block via the sacral notch approach for chronic pelvic pain: A retrospective CT-based observational study
summary: Chronic pelvic pain, commonly associated with endometriosis and other conditions including chronic pelvic inflammatory disease, uterine fibroids, and interstitial cystitis, can be effectively treated with superior hypogastric plexus block (SHGPB), a minimally invasive procedure that interrupts pain signal transmission through the superior hypogastric plexus. This CT-based study reveals important anatomical gender differences affecting the technical approach to SHGPB via the sacral notch method. Female patients demonstrate a significantly higher SHGPB index (1.96) compared to males (1.75), reflecting a wider pelvic anatomy that provides greater anatomical space for the procedure, while males have narrower pelves that may require modified operative techniques. Although puncture angle and distance remain stable between sexes, the sacral notch approach proves particularly advantageous for male patients whose narrower pelvic anatomy limits classical approaches. The SHGPB index emerges as a valuable imaging marker for preoperative evaluation and individualized procedural planning, enabling gender-specific optimization of this effective treatment for endometriosis-related pain and other chronic pelvic pain conditions.

title: Evaluation of robotic exposure among gynecological surgeons: results of survey from the young European advocates of robotic surgery (YEARS)
summary:
# Summary

This survey of young European gynecologic surgeons examining robotic surgery adoption reveals that surgeon confidence in robotic-assisted procedures is primarily driven by age over 35 years and regular operative exposure, specifically at least one dedicated robotic operating day per week or performing approximately 55+ procedures annually. Formal structured training through fellowships or industry-sponsored courses significantly enhances confidence levels compared to informal training alone. The study demonstrates that institutional surgical volume directly correlates with surgeon confidence and clinical outcomes, with departments performing high robotic case volumes and those with dedicated operative time more likely to harbor confident practitioners. A notable discrepancy exists between widespread robotic platform implementation and declining overall gynecologic surgical volumes, which paradoxically reduces trainee exposure to core procedures and diminishes confidence in performing traditional approaches. The findings underscore that cumulative experience combined with consistent operative practice are essential for developing proficiency, typically achieved after 20-50 cases depending on robotic platform type. The survey highlights that despite frequent robotic operating schedules in many departments, junior surgeons have limited participation, suggesting barriers to equitable training opportunities in this evolving surgical landscape.

title: The Psychological Impact of In Vitro Fertilization (IVF): A Gender Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that infertility and in vitro fertilization treatment constitute significant psychosocial stressors, with women experiencing substantially higher levels of psychological distress than men across multiple domains including anxiety, depression, and infertility-related distress. Critical stress points occur during oocyte retrieval and the waiting period before pregnancy testing, while the IVF planning phase is characterized by impaired couple adjustment, elevated perceived stress, and maladaptive coping patterns. Women engage more intensively in infertility-related activities such as information seeking and reflective thinking, which paradoxically associate with increased stress levels, whereas men generally report lower overall distress but remain vulnerable in specific psychosocial domains such as social functioning. Coping strategies significantly moderate psychological outcomes: maladaptive approaches including avoidance, self-blame, and passive coping correlate with higher depressive symptoms, while adaptive strategies such as problem-focused coping and social support seeking effectively reduce psychological burden. Younger individuals undergoing IVF face greater psychological and social challenges than older cohorts, and notably, strong emotional connection between partners is associated with improved treatment success rates. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive, gender-informed psychological interventions and coping skill development to mitigate the substantial mental health burden associated with fertility treatment.

title: Prevalence and Clinical Correlates of Fibromyalgia Screening Positivity in Patients with Inflammatory Bowel Disease
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of 274 inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients found that fibromyalgia (FM) screening positivity occurred in 18.6% of the cohort and identified FM as a distinct comorbidity rather than a manifestation of underlying IBD inflammation. FM and IBD appear to share common pathogenic mechanisms involving central sensitization driven by chronic nociceptive input and psychosocial factors, with both conditions showing genetic susceptibility as a contributing factor. Patients screening positive for FM were older, had higher comorbidity burden including thyroid disorders and vitamin D deficiency, but importantly showed no differences in IBD disease activity, inflammatory markers, or IBD phenotype severity compared to FM-negative patients. These findings underscore the clinical importance of distinguishing FM-related pain amplification from inflammatory pain, as FM confounds symptom-based assessments of IBD activity and may lead to unnecessary treatment escalation. The authors recommend incorporating brief FM screening tools into routine IBD clinical practice to improve diagnostic accuracy and avoid inappropriate intensification of immunosuppressive therapy for patients whose pain originates from central sensitization rather than active inflammation.

title: Second-trimester pregnancy loss after in vitro fertilization: risk factors and risk reduction
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of second-trimester pregnancy loss (STL) following in vitro fertilization identifies multiple maternal and procedural risk factors associated with adverse outcomes. Uterine factors, particularly adenomyosis, intrauterine scarring, and tuboperitoneal disease, emerge as the strongest predictors of STL, with adenomyosis and scarring each present in approximately 36% of loss cases compared to 6-13% in controls. Racial and ethnic disparities are notable, with Black and Hispanic patients showing significantly elevated STL risk independent of other factors. The predominant mechanism of loss involves preterm premature rupture of membranes and cervical incompetence, potentially mediated by underlying inflammatory processes in the uterus. Importantly, two evidence-based interventions can substantially reduce STL risk: elective single embryo transfer and preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy, both of which have been associated with dramatic reductions in loss rates. These findings underscore the importance of identifying and managing uterine pathology prior to embryo transfer while employing selective transfer strategies to optimize outcomes in IVF patients.

title: Adverse childhood experiences, obesity, and endometrial cancer: A case series and scoping review
summary: This case series and scoping review demonstrates a complex interconnection between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), obesity, and endometrial cancer risk, particularly in women. Exposure to ACEs—including psychological abuse, sexual abuse, household dysfunction, and bullying—significantly increases the risk of class 2 and 3 obesity in women and is associated with endometriosis and uterine fibroids, creating a pathway to endometrial cancer development. Obesity itself is one of the most well-established risk factors for endometrial cancer, with incidence patterns mirroring rising obesity prevalence, and is accompanied by substantial cardiometabolic and psychiatric comorbidities including diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and depression. Beyond increasing cancer risk, ACE exposure complicates endometrial cancer patient care, leading to delayed diagnosis at advanced stages and significant treatment-related distress, including trauma symptoms triggered by vaginal brachytherapy—particularly in patients with sexual trauma histories. The cohort exhibited remarkable medical complexity with multiple conditions including sleep apnea, chronic kidney disease, and eating disorders, suggesting that ACE and obesity interact to create a cascade of health vulnerabilities. These findings emphasize the necessity of trauma-informed gynecologic care and early screening for both ACE exposure and obesity to improve endometrial cancer outcomes and patient experiences during treatment.

title: Characteristics and evolution of pelvic floor structures in female patients aged over 40 years with constipation—a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined pelvic floor structural changes in women over 40 years with chronic constipation using transperineal ultrasound imaging. Multivariate analysis identified rectocele and uterine descent during Valsalva maneuver as independent risk factors for constipation, with rectocele present in 9.5% of constipated patients versus 5.3% of controls. Pelvic floor dysfunction—characterized by abnormal muscle coordination, hyper- or hypoactivity, and anatomical prolapse—emerged as a major mechanism underlying constipation, alongside contributions from dietary fiber deficiency, sedentary behavior, and psychological stress. Compared to non-constipated women, those with constipation demonstrated progressive structural deterioration including enlarged levator ani hiatus, increased perineal hypermobility, and greater bladder descent at two-year follow-up, whereas pelvic anatomy remained stable in non-constipated controls. The findings underscore how posterior compartment pathology (rectocele), anterior compartment changes (vesicocele, bladder descent), and pelvic floor dysfunction collectively contribute to constipation severity in aging women, suggesting that ultrasound-guided structural assessment could improve clinical management strategies for this prevalent condition.

title: Disparities in fatigue levels and dietary habits between men and women with inflammatory bowel disease: a comparative analysis with a control cohort
summary: This comparative analysis demonstrates that inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, is strongly associated with fatigue affecting approximately 72% of patients with active disease, with inflammation (measured by C-reactive protein and fecal calprotectin) serving as a key mechanistic driver. Women with IBD experience significantly greater fatigue burden than men, exhibiting lower FACIT-Fatigue scores and steeper declines in both overall and food-related quality of life. Multiple nutritional pathways contribute to IBD-associated fatigue, including vitamin and mineral deficiencies, protein inadequacy, and poor dietary habits characterized by excessive high-fat and high-sugar consumption, all of which disrupt metabolic homeostasis. Both men and women with IBD demonstrate suboptimal dietary habits compared to healthy controls, suggesting that dietary interventions represent a modifiable target for fatigue management. The study reveals complex interactions between inflammatory markers, dietary patterns, nutritional status, and psychosocial factors in determining fatigue severity, with handgrip strength serving as an objective correlate of fatigue severity. These findings highlight sex-based disparities in fatigue experience among IBD patients and underscore the multifactorial nature of IBD-associated fatigue.

title: Impact of maternal HIV infection on pregnancy and labor complication and perinatal health outcomes: a South African retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This South African retrospective study examined maternal HIV infection and COVID-19 pandemic impacts on pregnancy complications and perinatal outcomes. Maternal HIV infection was associated with significantly increased risks of preterm birth, low birthweight, intrauterine growth restriction, anemia, and hypertensive disorders, with pregnant women living with HIV experiencing two-to-ten times higher mortality risk during pregnancy and the postpartum period. While antiretroviral therapy (ART) effectively reduces mother-to-child transmission and improves maternal health, its use during pregnancy was associated with some adverse outcomes including preterm birth and fetal growth restriction. The COVID-19 pandemic substantially disrupted antenatal care attendance and hospital visits among HIV-infected women, creating additional challenges in HIV disease management through reduced access and movement restrictions. Despite these disruptions, effective viral suppression was maintained in most women on ART, though pandemic-related stress and delayed care-seeking contributed to elevated preterm delivery rates and higher sepsis incidence in HIV-infected pregnancies. The study underscores the critical importance of sustained antiretroviral coverage and uninterrupted antenatal care services for optimizing maternal and perinatal health outcomes in women living with HIV.

title: The effect of fetal movement awareness on birth outcomes among Somali migrant women – findings from a non-randomised intervention study in Sweden
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the Mindfetalness intervention—a maternal awareness program involving daily fetal movement monitoring from 28 weeks gestation—among Somali migrant women in Sweden, a population at higher risk for adverse outcomes including preventable stillbirth and inadequate antenatal care. The intervention significantly increased spontaneous vaginal birth rates (adjusted relative risk 1.06), with benefits particularly pronounced in multiparous women, though no statistically significant differences were found in other outcomes including Apgar scores, neonatal intensive care admission, or stillbirth rates. Somali-born women characteristically seek healthcare less frequently for decreased fetal movements compared to Swedish-born women, suggesting that culturally tailored, first-language education about fetal movement patterns could improve early detection of fetal distress and enable timely interventions. The intervention group had higher baseline rates of gynecological disease including endometriosis, which may have influenced birth outcomes. These findings suggest that enhancing fetal movement awareness through accessible, culturally appropriate interventions may improve maternal-neonatal outcomes in migrant populations, though larger studies are needed to confirm effects on serious complications like stillbirth.

title: Extreme ambient heat and outcomes of assisted reproduction in the continental United States, 1996 to 2018
summary: This study examined how extreme ambient heat during assisted reproductive technology (ART) cycles affects fertility outcomes across the continental United States from 1996 to 2018. The research found that exposure to extreme heat during folliculogenesis was associated with increased cycle cancellation and decreased live birth rates, with the most pronounced effects in Western regions where patients exposed to ≥15 days of extreme heat had 21% greater odds of cycle cancellation and 28% lower odds of achieving live birth. A 10°F increase in ambient temperature during folliculogenesis and ovarian stimulation was nationally associated with modest increases in cycle cancellation and decreases in live birth rates. The biological mechanism likely involves heat stress reducing oocyte quality through mechanisms well-documented in animal studies, with environmental heat exposure elevating temperatures that compromise oogenesis. Regional variations in heat sensitivity suggest that populations accustomed to extreme heat may have developed adaptive mechanisms, or that other environmental and socioeconomic factors in Western regions confound the relationship between temperature and ART outcomes.

title: Maternal Stress, Depression, Anxiety, and Participation in Care in Neonatal Semi-Intensive and Intensive Care Units: Results of a Cross-Sectional Study in Two Sri Lankan Hospitals
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of mothers in Sri Lankan neonatal intensive care units reveals that NICU admission of neonates—caused by conditions including prematurity, hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, sepsis, jaundice, and congenital anomalies—triggers substantial maternal psychological morbidity, with 73% of mothers exhibiting elevated stress, 87% showing depression, and over 77% experiencing state anxiety. Maternal stress, anxiety, and depression consequentially impair infant developmental outcomes and parent-infant relationships through behavioral disruption, while parental involvement in neonatal care emerges as a protective factor, with mothers achieving an Infant Parental Participation score of 20 or higher demonstrating significantly reduced stress and anxiety levels. The highest stress domain identified was parental role alteration, whereas technical caregiving tasks represented the lowest maternal participation area. Notably, 59% of mothers experienced concurrent depression, anxiety, and stress, and ethnicity influenced anxiety expression, with Tamil and Muslim mothers showing lower state anxiety compared to Sinhalese mothers, suggesting culturally-mediated psychological responses to neonatal hospitalization.

title: Post-COVID fatigue disproportionately affects women: evidence from the DEFEAT Corona cohort
summary:
# Summary

Post-COVID syndrome (PCS) affects approximately 6-10% of COVID-19 survivors and disproportionately impacts women, who report significantly higher rates of fatigue, brain fog, and sleep disturbances compared to men. The DEFEAT Corona cohort found that female PCS patients experience greater fatigue intensity, more severe brain fog, and substantially lower health-related quality of life than male counterparts. Notably, approximately two-thirds of menstruating PCS patients report cyclical worsening of fatigue symptoms linked to menstrual cycle fluctuations, with peripheral and endometrial inflammation contributing to this pattern through hormonal influences on energy levels, sleep, and cognitive function. The underlying mechanisms involve sex-specific autoimmune dysregulation including elevated autoantibody levels and B-cell reactivity, alongside neuroinflammatory processes and sex-differential activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. These findings underscore the need for gender-aware clinical approaches to PCS management and highlight reproductive health factors, including potential endometriosis comorbidity, as important considerations in understanding symptom severity and quality of life outcomes in female patients.

title: The hidden link between iron deficiency and celiac disease: a clinical perspective
summary: Celiac disease is an immune-mediated enteropathy triggered by gluten ingestion in genetically predisposed individuals carrying HLA-DQ2 and/or HLA-DQ8 haplotypes, in which both innate and adaptive immune responses drive intestinal inflammation and villous atrophy. This villous damage in the duodenum—the primary site of iron absorption—leads to reduced iron uptake and consequent iron deficiency, characterized by low ferritin and hepcidin levels and manifesting clinically as fatigue, hair loss, brittle nails, and angular cheilitis. The study identified a diagnostic challenge posed by seronegative individuals with Marsh 1 histology and permissive HLA genotypes (designated SSCDM1), who exhibit mucosal immune activation despite absent celiac-specific serological markers and resembled overt celiac disease in their iron depletion profiles and associated autoimmune comorbidities such as autoimmune thyroiditis. A strict gluten-free diet remains the only effective treatment, progressively reducing celiac-specific antibodies and allowing intestinal mucosal recovery. Celiac disease is also associated with serious complications including increased risk of intestinal lymphoma, and frequently presents asymptomatically or with atypical features, underscoring the importance of comprehensive clinical and immunological assessment for accurate diagnosis.

title: Asthma and Infertility: A Prospective Case–Control Study on Pregnancy and Live Birth Rates in Women With Asthma Undergoing Assisted Reproduction
summary: This prospective case-control study investigated the relationship between asthma and fertility outcomes in women undergoing assisted reproductive technologies (ART). Asthma, characterized by airway hyperresponsiveness, bronchial obstruction, and infiltration of inflammatory cells, was associated with reduced fertility and increased time to pregnancy, prompting greater utilization of ART procedures. Notably, despite the documented association between asthma and infertility in the general population, women with asthma who underwent IVF/ICSI or IUI treatments achieved clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates comparable to controls, suggesting that ART effectively circumvents the fertility challenges posed by asthma-related inflammation. Women with asthma required lower total follicle-stimulating hormone doses during ovarian stimulation and had higher body mass indices compared to controls. The findings indicate that while asthma independently impairs natural conception, likely through systemic inflammation affecting implantation processes, the condition does not compromise reproductive outcomes once assisted reproduction is employed, though the underlying mechanisms warrant further investigation.

title: Stories From Black Women in Iowa About Reproductive Health Care Experiences, Self‐Advocacy, and Recommendations for Change
journal: Health Expectations : An International Journal of Public Participation in Health Care and Health Policy
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study documents how structural racism and racial discrimination systematically undermine reproductive health outcomes for Black women in Iowa. Structural racism contributes directly to reproductive health disparities and poor health outcomes, while racism in clinical interactions causes dismissal of patient voices, discrimination, and paternalistic decision-making that erodes trust and generates iatrophobia—fear of healthcare providers. Black women in Iowa experience disproportionately high rates of maternal mortality, infertility, sexually transmitted infections, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including low birth weight and premature delivery, alongside lower rates of prenatal care initiation and cervical cancer screening. Racial discrimination by healthcare providers is associated with dramatically reduced odds of receiving provider explanations, having questions answered, or spending sufficient time in appointments. Study participants reported feeling physically and emotionally invisible, receiving inadequate information about their bodies and health conditions, and being presented with limited care options, experiences that paradoxically motivated greater self-advocacy but also fostered mistrust of providers and health systems. The findings underscore how systemic inequities and implicit biases—particularly in rural areas with fewer providers—perpetuate reproductive health disparities for Black women.

title: Preoperative predictive factors associated with severe postdischarge pain after ambulatory gynaecological laparoscopy: a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study identified four independent preoperative predictive factors for severe postdischarge pain following ambulatory gynaecological laparoscopy: younger age, preoperative pain, preoperative opioid use, and expectation of severe postoperative pain. Among 439 patients undergoing laparoscopic procedures (48.1% for endometriosis excision), 42.4% experienced severe pain after discharge despite multimodal pain prophylaxis including paracetamol, NSAIDs, dexamethasone, and regional anesthesia with propofol, remifentanil, and local bupivacaine infiltration. Preoperative opioid users demonstrated nearly 90% increased odds of severe postdischarge pain, likely due to opioid tolerance and opioid-induced hyperalgesia, while younger patients and those with existing pain conditions showed heightened pain responses possibly related to central sensitization and ongoing nociceptive input. Pain expectation emerged as a significant psychological predictor, with approximately one-third of patients anticipating severe postoperative pain, underscoring the role of cognitive factors in postoperative pain perception. The findings underscore that ambulatory surgery patients remain at substantial risk for severe postdischarge pain with limited access to medical support, suggesting that identifying high-risk patients preoperatively could enable targeted perioperative interventions to improve outcomes.

title: Sex‐Based Differences in Noncommunicable Diseases in Older Adults: Raising the Profile of Older Females
journal: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society
summary:
# Summary

Older females experience substantially higher rates of noncommunicable diseases, disability, and poor health outcomes compared to males, despite longer life expectancy. A striking sex disparity exists across multiple disease categories, including autoimmune diseases (affecting four of five patients), psychiatric conditions, and conditions like migraines, Alzheimer's disease, osteoarthritis, and osteoporosis, where genetic and molecular factors contribute to female predominance. Hormonal changes during the menopausal transition drive sex-specific pathology, including estrogen decline-associated depression and anxiety, accelerated bone density loss, and osteoporosis development. Females also present with atypical cardiovascular symptoms during myocardial infarction (nausea, vomiting, shortness of breath) and show higher prevalence of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, while reproductive-specific conditions including endometriosis remain important female health concerns. Socioeconomic disparities and language barriers further compound negative cardiovascular outcomes in older women, highlighting the need for sex-aware clinical approaches in geriatric care.

title: Sociodemographic and medical correlates of fertility-related quality of life in primary infertile Czech couples attending fertility clinics
summary:
# Summary

This study examined sociodemographic and medical factors influencing fertility-related quality of life in Czech couples attending fertility clinics, using the FertiQoL instrument to measure emotional, mind-body, relational, and social dimensions. Women reported significantly lower quality of life across all measured domains compared to men, and the specific cause of infertility emerged as a critical determinant, with men diagnosed with male-factor or mixed-factor infertility showing particular emotional and physical distress. Prior assisted reproductive technology experience was associated with reduced emotional and mind-body quality of life scores, while longer relationship duration paradoxically correlated with lower total, relational, and social quality of life scores. Socioeconomic factors, particularly higher household income and residence in mid-sized cities, were protective and associated with better quality of life outcomes. The findings underscore that infertility produces multifaceted psychological consequences including anxiety, depression, and sexual dysfunction that strain couple relationships, with effects intensified by treatment history and prolonged duration of infertility.

title: The Burden of Parkinson’s Disease Based on the GBD 2021
summary:
# Summary

Parkinson's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, causing early motor symptoms including tremor, dyskinesia, and rigidity, alongside later-stage non-motor manifestations such as constipation, anosmia, and memory impairment. Global PD burden has increased dramatically from 3.15 million cases in 1990 to 11.77 million by 2021, with projections reaching 25.2 million cases by 2050, driven primarily by population aging and demographic shifts. Age represents the strongest risk factor, with PD prevalence rising sharply after age 60 and peaking between 85-94 years, while both period and birth cohort effects demonstrate increasing relative risk over time. Geographic disparities are pronounced, with Southeast Asia, East Asia, and Oceania experiencing the most dramatic increases, and high-income regions including Taiwan, Canada, Israel, and Norway showing prevalence rates exceeding global averages by over 40 percent. The disease burden is quantified by substantial increases in disability-adjusted life years and mortality rates, positioning PD as one of the fastest-growing neurological disorders globally.

title: The value of a non-invasive bladder sensitivity paradigm in chronic pelvic pain
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome commonly co-occur with chronic pelvic pain, and bladder hypersensitivity appears to be a key mechanistic contributor to this symptom complex. Using a non-invasive bladder sensitivity paradigm that measures pain and urgency ratings during progressive bladder filling, patients with comorbid pain and bladder symptoms demonstrated significantly elevated pain intensity scores at all measured timepoints—first sensation, first urge, and maximum tolerance—compared to pain-only or control groups. Notably, patients with bladder pain syndrome showed heightened sensitivity even compared to those with pain and endometriosis alone, suggesting distinct underlying mechanisms of bladder dysfunction. Beyond bladder-specific findings, the pain-plus-bladder group exhibited higher scores on symptom questionnaires including interstitial cystitis severity indices and gastrointestinal symptom scales, along with increased state anxiety, indicating this is a systemic comorbidity rather than isolated bladder pathology. The paradigm successfully distinguished individuals with overt bladder symptoms and potentially identified those with "silent" bladder hypersensitivity, suggesting clinical utility for identifying bladder involvement in chronic pelvic pain populations.

title: Women’s midlife: the front line of Alzheimer prevention
summary:
# Summary

Female sex is the strongest risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer disease after advanced age, with menopause playing a critical mechanistic role in this heightened vulnerability. The decline in 17β-estradiol during menopause removes crucial neuroprotective effects, including reduced inflammation, suppressed tau phosphorylation, and protection against amyloid-β neurotoxicity, while concurrent elevations in follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone actively promote amyloidogenic processes. Postmenopausal women exhibit increased amyloid-β and tau burden alongside decreased cerebral glucose metabolism and gray matter volume, establishing a clear link between reproductive aging and AD pathology. The timing of menopause substantially influences dementia risk—later menopause after age 55 reduces AD risk by 33 percent, whereas early menopause and bilateral oophorectomy nearly double long-term dementia risk. Midlife menopause hormone therapy shows promise for reducing AD risk through estrogen receptor modulation, whereas late-life initiation appears neutral or harmful, suggesting a critical window for preventive intervention during the menopausal transition.

title: Occupational health challenges faced by female anaesthesiologists: a scoping review
summary: This scoping review identifies significant occupational health challenges specific to female anaesthesiologists, encompassing reproductive, musculoskeletal, and environmental health concerns. Pregnant anaesthesiologists face substantial exposure to ionising radiation, surgical smoke, waste anaesthetic gas, and ergonomic strain, necessitating strict exposure limits including maximum 2 ppm for halogenated agents and 25 ppm for nitrous oxide. Beyond pregnancy, female anaesthesiologists experience high rates of pregnancy complications or loss (65.1%), reproductive assistance utilization (11.6%), and menopausal symptoms affecting 80% of women, with 40% reporting work-impacting fatigue, brain fog, and irritability. The review documents female-specific health concerns including endometriosis, fibroids, abnormal uterine bleeding, postpartum mood disorders, and increased risks of carpal tunnel syndrome and multiple sclerosis among anaesthesia providers. Lactating anaesthesiologists face barriers including insufficient protected milk expression time, inadequate lactation facilities, and institutional stigma, while the review recommends minimum maternity leave of 6-8 weeks depending on delivery type and interventions such as replacing rigid laryngoscopes with flexible alternatives to prevent musculoskeletal injury. These findings underscore the need for comprehensive workplace policies addressing the unique occupational health vulnerabilities of female anaesthesiologists across their reproductive lifespan.

title: Women with chronic pelvic pain can be stratified using multimodal assessment
summary: Women with chronic pelvic pain (CPP) present with a complex constellation of comorbidities including significantly elevated fatigue, sleep disturbance, anxiety, depression, and pain catastrophizing compared to controls, alongside a history of childhood trauma. Multimodal assessment revealed that CPP patients exhibit both altered nociceptive processing—characterized by reduced thermal sensation and vibration detection but paradoxically elevated abdominal pressure pain thresholds—and heightened gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, indigestion, and constipation. Latent profile analysis identified three distinct CPP subgroups that were differentiated primarily by psychological and symptom questionnaires rather than physiological measures, with one cluster exhibiting predominantly higher-impact pain potentially associated with nociplastic pain mechanisms. These findings demonstrate that endometriosis-associated pain, bladder pain syndrome, and other secondary pelvic pain conditions present as heterogeneous phenotypes requiring stratification beyond traditional clinical diagnosis. The predominance of questionnaire-based rather than physiological differentiation suggests that psychological and psychosocial factors are critical for understanding and potentially treating distinct CPP subpopulations. This multimodal assessment approach provides a framework for personalizing treatment strategies based on individual patient phenotype rather than diagnosis alone.

title: A laboratory micro-CT technique is useful to visualize and characterize dermal skin components in a 3D manner
summary: This study demonstrates that X-ray phase-contrast micro-CT imaging effectively visualizes three-dimensional dermal skin architecture, revealing distinct collagen fiber organization patterns across patient populations. Patients with hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) display significantly disorganized collagen fiber bundles compared to healthy controls and those with postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) alone, who maintain more parallel collagen organization. POTS frequently co-occurs with hEDS and is associated with gastrointestinal dysfunction, autonomic neuropathy, and small fiber neuropathy with reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density, whereas gastrointestinal symptoms and extraintestinal manifestations are paradoxically more severe in POTS patients without hEDS/EDS. Patients with POTS and hEDS/EDS exhibit a particularly high disease burden, including endometriosis, fibromyalgia, anxiety, depression, gastrointestinal dysmotility, and sleep disturbances. The structural collagen abnormalities identified through micro-CT imaging correlate with the connective tissue dysfunction characteristic of EDS, which results from collagen gene mutations and explains the joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility observed in affected individuals.

title: PRAISE-H study exploring real-world challenges faced by reproductive-age patients with rheumatoid arthritis in Japan
summary:
# Summary

The PRAISE-H study examined reproductive challenges in Japanese women with rheumatoid arthritis (RA), revealing that high disease activity significantly contributes to prolonged time to pregnancy and delayed pregnancy onset, partly through elevated inflammatory cytokines that impair embryo implantation. While TNF inhibitors effectively maintain low disease activity during pregnancy and are compatible with conception, methotrexate—a cornerstone RA treatment—is contraindicated due to teratogenicity and miscarriage risk, forcing difficult medication transitions during pregnancy planning. Biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (bDMARDs) offer superior disease control and pregnancy compatibility but impose substantial financial toxicity that prevents many women from pursuing pregnancy planning. Beyond RA management, reproductive-age women with RA faced compounding healthcare gaps including low body mass index, limited access to gynecological care, and low cervical cancer screening rates, while reporting multifaceted pregnancy concerns spanning medication safety, advancing age, and work-life balance. The study underscores that optimizing fertility outcomes requires integrated management addressing both disease remission through appropriate biologics and systemic healthcare barriers.

title: The Invisible Burden: Examining the Impact of Exposure Misclassification in Epidemiologic Analyses of Uterine Fibroids
summary: Uterine fibroids are a common gynecologic condition with substantial morbidity, including heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic and back pain, anemia, and associations with hypertension, depression, and anxiety. The pathophysiology involves estrogen and progesterone regulation of fibroid growth, with proposed mechanisms including increased creatine kinase production that triggers vascular smooth muscle cell proliferation and elevated blood pressure, as well as fibroid-induced inflammation causing metabolic changes and blood vessel narrowing. This study emphasizes that exposure misclassification significantly biases epidemiologic findings, with self-reported fibroid detection showing the largest bias toward the null due to low sensitivity below 50%, while transvaginal ultrasound and pelvic MRI achieve 99% sensitivity with minimal bias. Notably, Black females have 2–3 times higher fibroid prevalence than White females, and low-sensitivity detection methods may fail to identify true effects in this population while identifying them in others, potentially masking important health disparities. The location and size of fibroids determine clinical presentation, with small submucosal fibroids causing heavy menstrual bleeding while large subserosal fibroids may remain asymptomatic. Quantitative bias analysis and sensitivity analyses are essential epidemiologic tools for addressing measurement error and ensuring accurate assessment of fibroid-associated health outcomes.

title: Hypophosphatasia: low penetrance of pathogenic and likely-pathogenic ALPL variants identified through an unselected biorepository
journal: Journal of Bone and Mineral Research
summary:
# Summary: Low Penetrance of Pathogenic ALPL Variants in Hypophosphatasia

Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a heritable multisystem disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the ALPL gene, which encodes tissue nonspecific alkaline phosphatase. While ALPL-positive individuals show low serum alkaline phosphatase activity and manifest mild symptoms—predominantly neurological (82%) and musculoskeletal (57%)—only 12.9% of ALPL variant carriers actually met clinical HPP diagnostic criteria, demonstrating remarkably low disease penetrance. The most common variant, c.571G>A, exhibits a modest reduction in enzyme activity and was found in 87.5% of ALPL-positive individuals without biochemical evidence of low serum ALP, suggesting limited clinical predictive value. More severe variants like c.346G>A display strong dominant negative effects and greater enzyme activity reduction, with inheritance pattern and allele severity together influencing phenotypic expression. Notably, ALPL-positive individuals showed increased risk for gait abnormalities compared to controls, while fatigue, arthritis, and dental problems occurred at similar frequencies between groups. The study underscores that pathogenic ALPL variants exhibit incomplete penetrance in population biorepositories, necessitating cautious interpretation of genetic findings and integration with biochemical and clinical assessments for HPP diagnosis.

title: Exercise Enjoyment and Exercise Addiction Risk Among Turkish Adults: Associations and Subgroup Differences in a Cross-Sectional Survey
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of Turkish adults examined how exercise enjoyment and exercise addiction risk relate to demographic, lifestyle, and psychological factors. Exercise enjoyment demonstrated strong associations with long-term adherence and exercise persistence and was inversely correlated with exercise addiction risk, suggesting that truly pleasurable exercise engagement represents an adaptive motivational pattern distinct from compulsive exercise behavior. Women, younger adults, those exercising 1-7 days per week, individuals with normal or overweight BMI, and those with better sleep quality reported higher enjoyment, while exercise addiction risk was elevated in women, younger adults, and those with postgraduate education or occasional alcohol use. Mechanistically, exercise enjoyment reflects flexible self-regulation driven by intrinsic motivation and autonomous choice, whereas exercise addiction risk is maintained by internal pressure, rigid persistence patterns, and controlled regulation independent of pleasure. The findings suggest that exercise program design features—including autonomy-supportive instruction, activity preference matching, and supportive social climates—can enhance enjoyment and promote adaptive engagement in adults, with implications for designing sustainable exercise interventions across diverse populations.

title: Endometriosis‐Associated Periodontal Disease: A Large Cohort Perspective Study
summary:
# Summary

This large cohort study establishes a significant association between endometriosis and periodontal disease, with severity correlating strongly to oral health deterioration. The connection is mediated by shared immunopathological mechanisms, including dysregulated cytokine profiles (elevated IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α), chronic low-grade inflammation, microbial dysbiosis, and impaired immune regulation. Advanced endometriosis stages (III-IV) show markedly higher periodontal disease perception scores, gingival bleeding, xerostomia, dental sensitivity, and tooth mobility compared to stage I disease. Notably, diagnostic delay substantially aggravates both systemic and oral manifestations, with delays exceeding six years associated with significantly elevated periodontal disease perception scores. Additionally, pharmacological and hormonal therapies used to treat endometriosis paradoxically exacerbate oral conditions through xerostomia, oral candidiasis, and mucosal inflammation, creating a compounding burden on oral health in advanced disease stages.

title: Inequalities in the health care system utilization for intrauterine device insertion
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals significant socioeconomic and structural barriers to intrauterine device (IUD) access within Brazil's primary health care system. Women with lower education levels and nulliparous women have substantially reduced access to IUDs and guidance on reproductive planning, while multiparous women and those with higher education utilize health care services more effectively. Hormonal IUDs are effective therapeutic options for endometriosis, adenomyosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, leiomyomatosis, and endometrial hyperplasia, yet systemic barriers limit their availability. Key obstacles include low awareness that primary health care units offer IUD insertion, restriction of procedures to specialist doctors rather than trained nurses, lengthy waiting times exceeding four months for 30.6% of patients, and failure to complete insertion in 25% of women who access services. Expanding nurse-led IUD insertion programs and improving reproductive planning counseling, particularly for disadvantaged populations, could substantially increase access to this long-acting reversible contraception method in Brazil.

title: Clinical profiles and modifiable risk factors for catheter-associated urinary tract infections in hospitalized obstetrics and gynecology patients: a case-control study
summary: This case-control study of obstetrics and gynecology inpatients identified multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors for catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTIs). The female urinary anatomy, particularly proximity of the urethra to the anus, facilitates pathogenic colonization, with Escherichia coli identified as the primary causative organism in 59.4% of cases, followed by Enterococcus faecalis and various gram-negative bacteria. Four key modifiable risk factors emerged from multivariate analysis: multiple catheterizations (≥2), discharge with an indwelling catheter, nutritional at-risk status upon admission, and hospitalization exceeding seven days, with multiple catheterizations conferring the highest risk (OR=16.642). Additional contributing factors included prolonged catheterization, improper insertion techniques, inadequate fluid intake, patient immobility, postmenopausal estrogen deficiency, and comorbid conditions such as diabetes and anemia. CAUTI patients predominantly presented with fever (85.5%), urinary occult blood (70.2%), and abnormal urinary leukocytes (55.7%), and infections were primarily managed with cefuroxime sodium, amikacin, and levofloxacin. These findings suggest that CAUTI prevention in gynecological patients should prioritize minimizing catheter use and duration, maintaining aseptic insertion protocols, and optimizing nutritional status.

title: THERAPEUTICS FOR SUBSTANCE-USING WOMEN: THE NEED TO ELUCIDATE SEX-SPECIFIC TARGETS FOR BETTER-TAILORED TREATMENTS
summary:
# Summary

This article examines sex-specific pharmacological targets for treating substance use disorders in women, emphasizing the critical role of hormonal and stress system dysregulation. Female rats and women demonstrate heightened hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis responses and greater substance self-administration compared to males, with elevated stress system markers including cortisol, corticotropin-releasing factor, and norepinephrine associated with increased intake of alcohol, cocaine, opioids, tobacco, and marijuana. Progesterone exerts protective effects by inhibiting subjective responses to stimulants and regulating emotion, yet substance-using women exhibit chronic progesterone deficiencies and attenuated estradiol-to-progesterone ratios across their menstrual cycle, predisposing them to anxiety, negative affect, and heightened drug craving particularly during follicular phases when progesterone is low. Menstrual cycle phase significantly modulates drug response and craving vulnerability, with depleted neurosteroid levels contributing to comorbid depression and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. The authors argue that effective treatments must account for these sex-specific hormonal factors and stress system sensitization to reduce relapse susceptibility and improve clinical outcomes in substance-dependent women.

title: MRI Findings of Ovarian Carcinosarcoma: A Retrospective Comparison With Endometrioid Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian carcinosarcoma is a rare, highly aggressive malignancy composed of both epithelial and mesenchymal components that predominantly affects postmenopausal women and is frequently associated with endometriosis (33% of cases). MRI imaging reveals distinctive features that help differentiate carcinosarcoma from endometrioid carcinoma, including irregular tumor margins, nodular solid components (100% of cases), predominant solid areas, and characteristic findings such as the mille-feuille sign, intratumoral hemorrhage (100%), necrosis (75%), and stained-glass-like cystic appearances. The tumors exhibit heterogeneous enhancement with restricted diffusion and often present as large masses (mean 13.6 cm) with advanced-stage disease at diagnosis, frequently showing peritoneal dissemination (66.7%) and distant metastases involving the omentum, peritoneum, and fallopian tubes. Although cytoreductive surgery followed by chemotherapy represents standard treatment, ovarian carcinosarcoma carries a poor prognosis due to chemotherapy resistance and the nonspecific nature of early imaging features that often delay preoperative diagnosis.

title: Prospective laparoscopic assessment of sigmoid elongation: association with constipation and abdominal pain
summary: This prospective laparoscopic study demonstrates that sigmoid elongation (dolichosigma) is independently associated with both constipation and abdominal pain in an unselected patient population, with longer sigmoid segments showing a dose-dependent relationship to constipation severity as measured by the Wexner Constipation Score. Mechanistically, elongated sigmoid loops increase intraluminal transit distance and promote fecal stasis, excessive water reabsorption, and colonic wall distension that slows peristaltic activity—a pathophysiologic mechanism confirmed in experimental models. Beyond simple mechanical obstruction, sigmoid elongation may cause partial hypoganglionosis and neuromotor degeneration, while also increasing mechanical tension and visceral hypersensitivity that contribute to abdominal pain symptoms. The study identifies female sex as a strong independent risk factor for constipation (adjusted OR 4.43), with arterial hypertension and lower BMI also showing significant associations. Notably, in women with rectosigmoid endometriosis, dolichocolon may blunt symptom relief following surgical treatment, suggesting that sigmoid elongation represents an important comorbidity complicating the clinical management of bowel endometriosis and potentially explaining refractory gastrointestinal symptoms in affected patients.

title: Effect of pericentric inversion of chromosome 9 on reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology: a propensity score–matched cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Pericentric inversion of chromosome 9 [inv(9)], a common chromosomal polymorphism found in 0.26–3.57% of the general population with higher prevalence in females and Asian populations, does not adversely affect assisted reproductive technology outcomes. A propensity score–matched cohort study comparing inv(9) carriers with karyotypically normal couples found comparable embryologic parameters including oocyte retrieval, maturation, fertilization, and blastocyst formation rates between groups. Notably, inv(9) carriers demonstrated slightly higher live birth rates in fresh embryo transfer cycles (46.4% vs controls), while pregnancy outcomes including biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, miscarriage, multiple births, and low birth weight were similar between groups in both fresh and frozen transfer cycles. Sex-stratified analysis showed that male and female inv(9) carriers had comparable reproductive outcomes, supporting the reclassification of inv(9) as a benign chromosomal variant that does not warrant reproductive counseling restrictions or special management in ART.

title: Sex Differences in Durability Following Heavy Intensity Cycling Exercise in Trained Athletes
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals significant sex differences in physiological responses and recovery following heavy-intensity cycling exercise in trained athletes. Females demonstrated superior durability and fatigue resistance, exhibiting smaller reductions in submaximal performance thresholds and greater preservation of contractile function compared to males, who showed more pronounced decrements in power at the gas exchange threshold and respiratory compensation point. These sex-based advantages in females appear mechanistically linked to enhanced fat oxidation, superior glycogen preservation, greater muscle perfusion and oxidative capacity, and a higher proportion of oxidative type I muscle fibers. During heavy-intensity exercise, males exhibited greater increases in heart rate, oxygen uptake, energy expenditure, and blood lactate accumulation, whereas females showed greater decreases in respiratory exchange ratio and lower muscle oxygen extraction with higher tissue oxygen saturation. The study controlled for hormonal influences by testing naturally menstruating females during the early follicular phase and standardizing assessment timing across different contraceptive users, thereby isolating intrinsic sex-based physiological differences in exercise durability and neuromuscular fatigue resistance.

title: Altered dorsal anterior insula functional connectivity underlying abnormal interoceptive accuracy awareness in migraine without aura
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: Migraine without aura is characterized by dysfunction in interoceptive processing—the brain's ability to sense internal bodily states—particularly involving altered connectivity in the dorsal anterior insula (dAI), a region critical for attention, cognitive regulation, and interoceptive awareness. While migraine patients paradoxically demonstrate heightened subjective interoceptive accuracy awareness compared to healthy controls, this is mediated by abnormally enhanced connectivity between the right dAI and bilateral precentral gyrus, a pathway that correlates with impaired objective interoceptive accuracy. Specific connectivity alterations in this network relate to migraine severity: diminished dAI-pallidum connectivity associates with increased headache frequency, while heightened dAI-middle temporal gyrus connectivity correlates with greater headache intensity. The posterior-to-anterior insula gradient reveals that while the posterior insula generates primary visceral signal representations, the dAI reprocesses these signals through cognitive and attentional mechanisms that become dysregulated in migraine. These altered connectivity patterns may serve as neuroimaging biomarkers for migraine, and therapeutic interventions targeting interoceptive function—including body-directed therapies and noninvasive auricular vagus nerve stimulation—show promise in symptom reduction by normalizing interoceptive processing.

title: Determinants of Folate and Vitamin B12 Deficiencies in Women of Reproductive Age: Insights from the 2018 National Nutrition Survey of Pakistan
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of Pakistan's 2018 National Nutrition Survey reveals that folate and vitamin B12 deficiencies are prevalent among women of reproductive age, with both micronutrients being critical for maintaining maternal health and supporting normal reproductive functions. Both deficiencies are strongly associated with anemia, which in turn impairs ovulation, compromises endometrial receptivity, and causes menstrual irregularities—outcomes particularly concerning for reproductive outcomes. Additionally, folate deficiency is linked to serious pregnancy complications including neural tube closure defects, low birth weight, and stunting, while B12 deficiency is associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes and increased fatigue and cardiovascular complications when left untreated. Geographically and sociodemographically, combined deficiencies are significantly higher in rural populations and certain provinces like Baluchistan and Gilgit-Baltistan, while overweight/obese women, those from smaller families, and pregnant women show elevated B12 deficiency risk. The study underscores substantial nutritional shortcomings in Pakistani women's diets, highlighting the need for targeted public health interventions to ensure adequate intake of animal products (for B12) and plant foods including leafy greens and fruits (for folate) to prevent serious reproductive and maternal health complications.

title: The Role of Surgeon Volume in the Relationship Between Race and Surgical Modality for Hysterectomy for Benign Indications
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that racial disparities in hysterectomy surgical modality for benign gynecological conditions, including endometriosis-related pathology, are substantially mediated by surgeon volume. Black patients are treated by low-volume surgeons at significantly higher rates (13.9% versus 9.7% for White patients), with a 46% greater odds ratio, and low-volume surgeons are 2.2 times more likely to perform abdominal rather than minimally invasive procedures. While surgeon volume accounts for only 2.7-3.1% of the racial disparity in surgical approach, it remains a key determinant; high-volume surgeons adopt minimally invasive surgery more consistently, conferring benefits including reduced morbidity, shorter hospital stays, and lower pain and readmission rates compared to abdominal approaches. Notably, low-volume surgeons disproportionately treat Black patients with benign indications including chronic pelvic pain, uterine bleeding, and leiomyomas, suggesting structural barriers—including inequitable insurance coverage, geographic availability of minimally invasive capabilities, and referral bias—combine with health weathering effects to perpetuate these disparities at a population level.

title: Functional co-activation of the default mode network in APOE ε4-carriers: A replication study
summary:
# Summary

This neuroimaging replication study demonstrates that APOE ε4-carriers exhibit significantly increased co-activation of the default mode network (DMN), particularly in the medial temporal lobe, medial prefrontal cortex, and retrosplenial cortices. APOE ε4-carriers showed heightened hippocampal task activation during memory encoding of subsequently recollected items, with enhanced functional activation in facial recognition-related areas, and this activation pattern correlated positively with polygenic risk scores incorporating APOE variants. The structural alterations observed in APOE ε4-carriers include decreased hippocampal volume and entorhinal cortex thickness, alongside altered resting-state network activation. These findings are clinically relevant because DMN regions are among the first brain areas to accumulate amyloid-beta and undergo neurodegeneration, and abnormal DMN deactivation patterns correlate with progression from normal aging to mild cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease. The APOE ε4-allele significantly elevates Alzheimer's disease risk with odds ratios of 3-15 for heterozygotes, contrasting with the protective effect of the ε2-allele, underscoring the critical role of this genetic variant in brain network dysfunction and neurodegeneration.
